An outcome is a result that also carries its uncertainty, a stated confidence level, and a method statement — 9.76 plus or minus 0.14 meters per second squared at 95% confidence, computed by partial derivative propagation. The distinction matters because the

rs per second squared at 95% confidence, computed by partial derivative propagation. The distinction matters because the outcome is what allows comparison. Without the uncertainty bounds, I cannot determine whether 9.76 agrees with a model that predicts 9.81. With the bounds, I can make a defensible quantitative argument. From this point forward, every numerical claim you make in this course must be expressed as an outcome. ================================================================================ slide 7: Uncertainty Is Mandatory — Here Is Why (Image: A horizontal number line showing a model prediction at a fixed point — vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction: 42.3 N·m." Two experimental outcomes are shown as horizontal bars indicating uncertainty ranges. Outcome A: narrow

tion: 42.3 N·m." Two experimental outcomes are shown as horizontal bars indicating uncertainty ranges. Outcome A: narrow bar centered at 44.1, labeled "44.1 ± 0.4 N·m — gap exceeds uncertainty, significant discrepancy." The bar does not overlap the model prediction line. Outcome B: wider bar also centered at 44.1, labeled "44.1 ± 2.8 N·m — agreement within uncertainty." This bar overlaps the model prediction line. Below both: "Same measured value — completely different engineering conclusions." A bold caption: "Uncertainty is not a footnote. It is the argument.") Talking Point: Here is the most important visual in this lecture. Both outcomes show the same measured value — 44.1 Newton-meters. The model predicts 42.3. Do they agree? The answer depends entirely on the uncertainty. In Outcome

— 44.1 Newton-meters. The model predicts 42.3. Do they agree? The answer depends entirely on the uncertainty. In Outcome A, the uncertainty is tiny — the gap between 44.1 and 42.3 is much larger than the bounds, and I have a significant discrepancy I need to explain. In Outcome B, the uncertainty is larger — the gap falls within the bounds, and agreement is defensible. The same number, measured by the same instrument, leads to completely different engineering conclusions depending on the uncertainty. This is why uncertainty is not optional in this course, and it is why professional standards like ASME V&V 10-2006 require it. Uncertainty is not a sign of weakness. It is the argument. ================================================================================ slide 8: Where Phase I

is the argument. ================================================================================ slide 8: Where Phase I Sits in the AMVF (Image: The full AMVF flowchart from slide 4, now with a gold shaded overlay covering the Physical Modeling Branch nodes (Physical Realization, Experimental Measurement, Data Reduction, Experimental Outcomes) and the Quantitative Comparison node. The Mathematical Modeling Branch is grayed out with a label "Given for now." A large bracket on the right side labels the shaded region "Phase I — Weeks 1–5: Execution & Quantification." Smaller annotations mark subsequent phases along the left branch: "Phase II — Weeks 6–10: Add predictive modeling" and "Phase III — Weeks 11–15: Design from scratch." The progression left-to-right in the AMVF corresponds to

e modeling" and "Phase III — Weeks 11–15: Design from scratch." The progression left-to-right in the AMVF corresponds to increasing student ownership.) Talking Point: For the next five weeks — Phase I of this course — your job is to walk the right branch of the AMVF with discipline. The mathematical model is given to you. Your job is to take the physical phenomenon, design and execute the measurement, reduce the data, propagate your uncertainty, and produce an experimental outcome that can be compared to the model prediction. You are not designing the experiment from scratch yet — that is Phase III. You are not developing the mathematical model independently — that is Phase II. But Phase I is not just a warmup. The physical modeling branch is where experimental credibility is earned or

Phase II. But Phase I is not just a warmup. The physical modeling branch is where experimental credibility is earned or lost. A perfectly good mathematical model cannot be validated by a carelessly executed experiment. Phase I builds the foundation everything else rests on. ================================================================================ slide 9: The AMVF Connects to Professional Engineering Standards (Image: The AMVF flowchart shown as a central hub with three satellite documents connected by arrows labeled "governed by." Satellite 1 (top): ASME V&V 10-2006 — shown as a formal document cover with subtitle "Guide for V&V in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer." Satellite 2 (bottom-left): AIAA Measurement Standards — shown as a document with the AIAA logo

amics and Heat Transfer." Satellite 2 (bottom-left): AIAA Measurement Standards — shown as a document with the AIAA logo placeholder. Satellite 3 (bottom-right): ASME PTC Measurement Uncertainty Standards — shown as a document cover. Below all three: a caption reading "The AMVF is not a course diagram. It is a professional framework grounded in published standards." Clean professional layout.) Talking Point: The AMVF is not something invented for this class. It reflects a framework that appears throughout professional aerospace and mechanical engineering and that is grounded in published engineering standards. The primary anchor for this course is ASME V&V 10-2006 — the American Society of Mechanical Engineers' Guide for Verification and Validation in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat

American Society of Mechanical Engineers' Guide for Verification and Validation in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer. Its procedural requirements are specific to computational modeling, but its conceptual vocabulary — verification, validation, model adequacy, uncertainty — has been widely adopted as the standard framework language across both computational and experimental engineering, which is why it anchors this course. You will be assigned a reading on this document this week, and it will be a reference throughout the course. The other standards on this slide — AIAA measurement standards, ASME measurement uncertainty standards — will be cited as we progress through the semester, and by Phase III you will be required to reference them in your V&V plan. Professional

we progress through the semester, and by Phase III you will be required to reference them in your V&V plan. Professional engineers do not make up their validation methodology from scratch — they anchor it to standards that the broader community recognizes. ================================================================================ slide 10: The Experimentally Informed Model (Image: A cyclical three-node loop diagram. Node 1 (top): "Mathematical Model" — an equation displayed, e.g., F = ma with some terms highlighted. Arrow down-right to Node 2 (right): "Experiment" — a test article with sensors, arrows labeled "predicts expected outcome." Arrow down-left to Node 3 (bottom): "Experimental Outcome" — a graph with data points and uncertainty bars alongside a model line, labeled

Node 3 (bottom): "Experimental Outcome" — a graph with data points and uncertainty bars alongside a model line, labeled "measured, with uncertainty." Arrow up-left back to Node 1, labeled "informs and updates model." The loop conveys iteration. Inset in the lower right corner: a small "before/after" graphic showing a model prediction curve (dashed) that initially misses the data, then an updated curve (solid) that passes through the data within uncertainty — labeled "Experimentally Informed Model.") Talking Point: There is a common misconception I want to address on the first day: the goal of an experiment is not to confirm the model. The goal is to learn from the comparison. When your experimental outcome disagrees with the model prediction, that discrepancy is not a failure — it is

omparison. When your experimental outcome disagrees with the model prediction, that discrepancy is not a failure — it is information. It tells you what physics the model is missing, or what physical effect you did not account for in your experimental setup. The most valuable output of the right branch of the AMVF is not a verdict of "confirmed" or "rejected" — it is an experimentally informed model, one that has been updated based on what the data revealed. For example, if you measure a pendulum's period and find it increases at larger amplitudes, the data tells you the simple-pendulum model is missing an amplitude-dependent physics term — that is the experimentally informed model insight. This concept runs through all five weeks of E1 and becomes the central deliverable in E2 and E3. In

ormed model insight. This concept runs through all five weeks of E1 and becomes the central deliverable in E2 and E3. In the first lab session this week, you will begin designing your canned experiment with this outcome explicitly in mind. ================================================================================ slide 11: What the Next Five Weeks Look Like (Image: A horizontal five-column table, one column per week. Week 1: "Framework + Design" — AMVF icon, experiment design icon. Week 2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma icon, MATLAB icon. Week 3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two curves compared icon, validation metric symbol. Week 4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon showing all steps connected. Week 5: "Cross-Prediction + Transition" — two students exchanging experiment packets icon,

showing all steps connected. Week 5: "Cross-Prediction + Transition" — two students exchanging experiment packets icon, bridge icon. Beneath each column: the primary deliverable for that week in smaller text. A gold arc labeled "Phase I: Execution and Quantification" spans all five columns. Each week's column is visually connected to the next by a right-pointing arrow.) Talking Point: Here is the Phase I arc at a glance. This week you design your experiment and annotate an AMVF diagram. Next week you acquire the mathematical tools for uncertainty quantification — partial derivatives, Monte Carlo, confidence intervals — and begin collecting data on your assigned canned experiment. In Week 3 you learn how to make a quantitative comparison argument using a named validation metric. Week 4

d experiment. In Week 3 you learn how to make a quantitative comparison argument using a named validation metric. Week 4 integrates everything into a complete pipeline from hypothesis to AMVF annotation — that is the biggest report of E1. Then in Week 5 you do something that changes how you think about experiments: before anyone releases the data, you construct a predictive model for an experiment you did not run and commit to a prediction in writing. That is the cognitive bridge into Phase II. Each week builds directly on the last — and you cannot skip steps. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Classify This Claim (Image: A single quote box on a clean white background, styled to look like an excerpt from an

ent — Classify This Claim (Image: A single quote box on a clean white background, styled to look like an excerpt from an engineering report. The text reads: "We ran the finite element simulation and compared the deflection predictions to our physical test results. The predictions agreed with the measurements to within 2.5%. The structural model is verified." Below the quote, a single prompt in bold: "Is this claim defensible? What, if anything, is wrong with it?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause — allow student responses.] The key issue is vocabulary: agreement with physical test data is validation, not verification. Verification checks whether the code correctly solves the equations — it is an internal consistency test. Validation checks whether the model

hether the code correctly solves the equations — it is an internal consistency test. Validation checks whether the model correctly represents physical reality — it requires comparison to physical evidence. This claim has done exactly the right experiment and reached exactly the wrong conclusion about what it demonstrates. A test engineer reading this report would flag the word "verified" immediately. If you can catch that error in two minutes on a report you have never seen before, you are doing test engineering. That is the skill this course is building. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure The Aerospace Modeling and Validation Framework is not a diagram you memorize and move on from. It is the decision-making structure that a

ling and Validation Framework is not a diagram you memorize and move on from. It is the decision-making structure that a test engineer returns to every time they need to argue that a result is credible — when they design an experiment, when they argue for or against agreement between data and model, and when they plan a V&V strategy for a complex system. Today you have seen where this course sits in that structure, why verification and validation are different things, and why uncertainty is not optional but mandatory. Over the next fifteen weeks you will internalize all of it — not by memorizing the framework but by using it, week after week, to make defensible engineering claims about physical systems. That is the skill employers pay for. That is what we are building together. Welcome to

ng claims about physical systems. That is the skill employers pay for. That is what we are building together. Welcome to Aerospace Experimental Methods. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - Test engineer professional identity framing planted prominently in slides 1, 3, and 12 per blueprint instruction. - ASME V&V 10-2006 named explicitly in slides 7 and 9 per instructor_clarification_notes change #12. - "Experimentally informed model" introduced in slide 10 per change #5. - AMVF two-branch structure, verification vs. validation, outcomes vs. results all grounded in Conceptual_Arc.md and Lecture_Layout_iteration1.md. - Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment +

ounded in Conceptual_Arc.md and Lecture_Layout_iteration1.md. - Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 12 (Evaluator Moment — classify V/V claim). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 9): Slide 9 talking point — added sentence acknowledging that V&V 10-2006 is CFD-specific in procedural scope but its conceptual vocabulary is broadly adopted across experimental engineering. - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 5 talking point — added concrete V/V example (correct equations + wrong boundary conditions = verified but not validated). Slide 10 talking point — added concrete experimentally informed model example (pendulum period increases with amplitude → model missing amplitude-dependent term).

pages.title: L02
pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Lecture L02 E1_W1_L2 — Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ========================================... E1_W1_L2 — Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design Talking Point: In the first lecture, I introduced the AMVF and showed you where measurement sits in the framework — on the physical modeling branch. Now I want to go deeper into what measurement actually is. Not how to press buttons on a sensor, but what a sensor is really doing: what it reports, what it infers, and what it silently assumes. And in the second half of this lecture, I'll introduce the formal vocabulary of experiment design — the language you will use in your Phase 1 design deliverable this week when you commit to a hypothesis and a procedure for your assigned canned experiment before any data collection begins. ================================================================================ slide 2: Measurement Is Model Realization (Image: A two-layer diagram on a clean white background. Top layer labeled "Physical Reality" shows a schematic of a physical system — a rotating wheel with angular velocity arrows, labeled "True Angular Velocity: ω." Bottom layer labeled "Sensor Output" shows a voltage-vs-time trace with visible noise and a slight baseline offset, labeled "Recorded Signal: 2.34 V ± noise." Between the two layers, a downward arrow passes through a gray box labeled with four stages: Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Analog-to-Digital Conversion → Calibration Equation. A red annotation at the bottom reads "This is not the physical quantity — it is a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this lecture, and I want it to land early: measurement is not data collection. Measurement is model realization. When you place a sensor on a structure or a system, you are not directly recording a physical truth — you are producing an inference about the physical world through a chain of transduction, conditioning, digitization, and calibration assumptions. Every step in that chain introduces error. Every step reflects a modeling choice. The voltage you read on your screen is not the angular velocity or the force or the temperature — it is the output of a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what your measurement is actually telling you. The physical modeling branch of the AMVF starts here — in this chain. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Transducer Chain (Image: A horizontal five-box chain diagram on a light background, boxes connected by right-pointing arrows. Box 1: "Physical Phenomenon" — icon of a force arrow on a surface. Box 2: "Transducer Element" — icon of a sensor element converting mechanical energy to electrical, labeled "e.g., strain gauge → resistance change." Box 3: "Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform being discretized into stepped digital levels, with arrow labeled "sampling rate matters here." Box 5: "Recorded Value" — a number in a spreadsheet cell. A red callout below Box 2 reads "Each stage introduces its own error source." A separate callout at Box 4 reads "Sampling and aliasing: Phase II, Week 7." The chain runs left to right across the full width of the slide.) Talking Point: The transducer chain is the sequence of physical and electronic processes that converts a real-world quantity into a recorded number. It starts at the phenomenon you care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in between is a transformation, and every transformation can introduce error. The transducer converts energy from one form to another: a piezoelectric crystal produces voltage in response to strain, a thermocouple generates voltage proportional to temperature difference, a MEMS accelerometer changes capacitance in response to inertial force. Signal conditioning amplifies, filters, and excites. The analog-to-digital converter discretizes the continuous signal. I will not go deep into sampling theory today — that is Week 7 in Phase II. But I want you to see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause. ================================================================================ slide 4: What We Measure vs. What We Infer (Image: A two-column diagram. Left column header: "What the Sensor Reports" — a blue box showing "Voltage: 2.34 V" with a voltmeter icon. Right column header: "What We Care About" — a gold box showing "Force: 14.7 ± 0.4 N" with a force arrow icon. Between the columns, a downward-pointing conversion block contains: "Calibration Equation: F = k·V + b" and below it "Assumptions: linear response, stable calibration constants, constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the assumptions in the conversion." An upward arrow from the right column back to the left with a dashed line asks: "If one assumption breaks — which column is wrong?") Talking Point: Sensors almost never directly measure the quantity you care about. A strain gauge measures resistance change. A thermocouple measures voltage. A pressure transducer measures membrane deflection. What you actually want is strain, temperature, or pressure. The conversion from what you measure to what you infer is not automatic — it is a calibration equation built on physical assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the electrical excitation fluctuates — the inference breaks too, even though the raw voltage reading is perfectly accurate. This is why calibration is not optional, and it is why I will ask you in every lab report to state explicitly what your sensor measured versus what you inferred from that measurement. The distinction is the beginning of experimental honesty. ================================================================================ slide 5: Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies (Image: A four-quadrant target diagram. Each quadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster shifted far from center, labeled "consistent but wrong." Top-right: "Low Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster near center, labeled "ideal." Bottom-left: "High Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster far from center, labeled "wrong and inconsistent." Bottom-right: "Low Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster near center, labeled "inconsistent but not systematically wrong." Below each quadrant: the remedy — "Calibration" (top-left), "No action needed" (top-right), "Calibration + more measurements" (bottom-left), "More measurements + isolation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.) Talking Point: Bias and precision are two distinct properties of a measurement system, and they require completely different interventions. Bias is a systematic offset — every reading is shifted by a consistent amount in the same direction. A poorly zeroed load cell, a thermocouple with a bad reference junction, a sensor mounted with mechanical preload — these all introduce bias. Bias can sometimes be corrected by calibration against a known reference, but only if you have that reference. Precision is about scatter — how much do repeated measurements of the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all biased. Low precision means your measurements scatter widely. Knowing which problem you have determines the solution: bias problems call for better calibration; precision problems call for more repetition, better isolation, and better experimental control. You will characterize both in your Phase 1 experiment. ================================================================================ slide 6: Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source (Image: A cross-sectional engineering sketch of a sensor mounted in a physical system. Three annotation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point, thermocouple reads lower than true fluid temperature." (2) "Mounting stress — over-tightening changes the mechanical boundary condition, altering strain at the gauge location." (3) "Electromagnetic interference — signal wire runs near a motor power lead, inducing 60 Hz noise into the measurement channel." The sketch is schematic and black-and-white with red callout arrows. A text note at the bottom: "Installation errors often exceed the sensor specification error. They are design choices, not random events.") Talking Point: A sensor that has been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field. Installation effects are the category of errors introduced by how and where a sensor is placed in the physical environment, and they are chronically underestimated by students and sometimes by professionals. Conduction error occurs when the sensor body conducts thermal energy away from the measurement point, pulling the reading toward ambient temperature. Mounting stress changes the mechanical state at the sensor location — a classic problem with strain gauges on thin or flexible structures. Electromagnetic interference is a signal contamination problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in aerospace laboratory work, and your experimental design this week must anticipate them before you take the first reading. If you discover an installation effect after the data is collected, you have a confound — not a measurement. ================================================================================ slide 7: Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A calibration curve graph on a clean white background. X-axis labeled "Known Reference Input (True Value)" with regular tick marks. Y-axis labeled "Sensor Output (Raw Units)." Two curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual Measured Response." The region between the curves is highlighted in yellow and labeled "Calibration Residual." Below the graph, a best-fit equation: "Output = a·Input + b, R² = 0.9992." In the bottom-right corner, a red text box: "Calibration adjusts the sensor output to match a known reference. It corrects bias. It does NOT validate a model.") Talking Point: Calibration is the process of comparing a sensor's output to a known reference standard and using that comparison to derive a characterization equation — a scale factor, an offset, a polynomial fit — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If your force sensor reads five percent high across its full range, calibration corrects that. Calibration is a measurement activity — it improves the fidelity of what the sensor reports about the physical world. I want to be explicit about what calibration is not: it is not validation. Calibration tells you the sensor is correctly reporting the physical quantity. Validation tells you whether your model accurately predicts system behavior. These are different claims about different things, and we will develop this distinction carefully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model. ================================================================================ slide 8: Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference (Image: A fishbone cause-and-effect diagram. The central horizontal spine points to a box labeled "Measured Outcome." Four angled branches extend from the spine, each labeled: "Environment" (sub-causes: temperature drift, humidity, ambient vibration), "Instrumentation" (sub-causes: sensor drift, noise floor, calibration age), "Operator" (sub-causes: positioning variability, recording inconsistency, timing errors), "Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch is clean and labeled in a consistent font. A red callout at the center node reads "Confounders produce apparent effects that are not caused by the independent variable.") Talking Point: A confounding factor is anything that causes your dependent variable to change for reasons other than the independent variable you're manipulating. If you're measuring the period of a pendulum while someone opens a window, and the airflow changes the drag on the pendulum bob, that's a confound. If you're measuring structural deflection and the temperature in the lab changes between trials, altering the material modulus, that's a confound. Confounders are dangerous because they can produce real, repeatable, statistically consistent apparent effects that have nothing to do with the phenomenon you are studying. The antidote to confounding is experimental design: controlled conditions, documented environment, randomized trial order, and careful isolation of the variable you are trying to manipulate. One of the required elements of your Phase 1 design deliverable this week is to identify at least one confounding factor in your assigned experiment before you run it — because after you have collected the data is the wrong time to remember them. ================================================================================ slide 9: Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary (Image: A structured three-section diagram on a clean white background, each section a labeled card. Card 1: "Hypothesis" — template text: "If [independent variable] is changed by [specific amount or direction], then [dependent variable] will [specific expected response], because [physical reasoning in one sentence]." Card 2: "Variables" — a three-column table with column headers: Independent Variable (what you change), Dependent Variable (what you measure), Controlled Variables (what you hold fixed). Card 3: "Confounders" — a short bullet list labeled "Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented." Below all three cards: a note reading "All three elements are required in the Phase 1 design deliverable this week." The cards are visually distinct with subtle color coding.) Talking Point: Formal experiment design requires specific vocabulary, and I want to give you that vocabulary precisely. A hypothesis is a specific, falsifiable, causal statement — it is not a question, it is not a guess, and it is not a vague expectation. An independent variable is the quantity you intentionally change. A dependent variable is the quantity you measure in response. Controlled variables are the quantities you hold constant so they do not contaminate the relationship you are studying. Together these three elements define the scope of the experiment — they tell you and everyone who reads your report what you were actually testing. This week's Phase 1 design deliverable requires all three, along with a hypothesis and at least one identified confounder. Come to the lab session this week ready to write these down for your assigned experiment, because that design work is what drives everything that follows. ================================================================================ slide 10: Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X: "If we change the input voltage to the circuit, the measured power will change." Below, three red annotations: "Direction unspecified," "No physical reasoning," "Not falsifiable in a useful sense." Right box, labeled "Strong Hypothesis" with a green checkmark: "If input voltage is increased from 3V to 9V while resistance is held constant, measured electrical power will increase by a factor of nine, because power is proportional to voltage squared (P = V²/R) for a fixed resistance." Below, three green annotations: "Specific quantitative prediction," "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual comparison.) Talking Point: The quality of your hypothesis determines the quality of your experiment. Let me show you exactly what I mean. The weak hypothesis — "if we change the input voltage, the power will change" — is technically true but scientifically useless. It predicts a directional change without specifying how much, and it gives no physical reasoning. If the power changes by five percent or by nine hundred percent, both confirm the weak hypothesis. The strong hypothesis specifies a quantitative prediction, states the physical mechanism, and is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of three-fold instead of nine-fold, I have a discrepancy I need to explain — and explaining it will teach me something. The strong hypothesis is the one worth testing. In your experiment design this week, your hypothesis must commit to a mechanism, not just a direction. ================================================================================ slide 11: Connecting Your Design to the AMVF (Image: The AMVF flowchart from Lecture 1, now with five sticky-note style annotations attached to specific nodes. At "Physical Realization": "Your hypothesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and calibration procedure." At "Data Reduction": "Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation." At "Experimental Outcomes": "Your measured values with uncertainty stated." At "Quantitative Comparison": "Your validation metric applied to outcomes vs. model." The rest of the diagram is lightly grayed. A label below the figure reads "Phase 1 design deliverable: annotate this diagram with your actual decisions.") Talking Point: Before you leave this lecture, I want to draw the explicit connection between everything we just discussed and the AMVF from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection, installation decisions, and calibration plan map to the instrumentation node. Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation map to data reduction. Your final reported values with uncertainty map to experimental outcomes. And your comparison to the predictive model maps to the quantitative comparison node. The Phase 1 design deliverable this week asks you to annotate an AMVF diagram with your actual design decisions at each node. That exercise is not bureaucratic — it is the most direct way to check whether every measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Is This Hypothesis Testable? (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's submitted hypothesis: "The motor will produce more torque at higher input voltage." Below the quote, a single prompt in bold: "What makes this hypothesis not testable as stated? How would you fix it to make it defensible?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this hypothesis. [Pause — allow student responses.] The claim is technically true but scientifically useless: "more torque" could mean 0.001% more or 500% more — both would confirm it. There is no quantitative prediction, no stated physical mechanism, and no threshold that a measurement could clearly contradict. A defensible version commits to all three: "If input voltage is increased from 12V to 24V while load resistance is held constant, motor output torque will increase by a factor of four, because in a DC motor under constant winding resistance and no magnetic saturation, torque scales as the square of the applied voltage." That version names the mechanism, commits to a specific ratio, and can be clearly contradicted by a measurement showing a factor of 1.8 or a factor of 10. Notice: the strong hypothesis taught you something even before the experiment ran — you had to know the physics to write it. That is the point. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure You now have two foundational layers for this course: the AMVF gives you the structural map, and measurement fundamentals give you the language for what happens at every node on that map. Measurement is not passive data collection — it is an active chain of physical and electronic modeling choices that each carry their own error. Designing an experiment means anticipating that chain before you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are inferring, and naming the confounders before they corrupt your result. This week's design deliverable is intentionally simple — the discipline of doing it carefully is not. The habits you build this week — hypothesis-first, confounder-aware, AMVF-connected — are the habits of a test engineer, and this is where they begin. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). References "assigned canned experiment" and "Phase 1 design deliverable" per Iteration 5 terminology — no "pendulum" as the specific experiment example. P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example, connecting naturally to the W2 Lecture 2 Lab 1 activity without introducing it early. Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced lightly in Slide 7 — tagged as "Week 3" for the deeper treatment, per blueprint continuity. Slide 11 explicitly connects to the annotated AMVF deliverable from the W1 lab session. Confounder identification requirement in Slide 8 and 9 matches the Phase 1 required elements listed in the W1 lab activity blueprint. Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 12 (Evaluator Moment — hypothesis falsifiability). V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 7 talking point — replaced "correction equation" with "characterization equation" to avoid implying the sensor was broken (characterization describes the sensor's mapping, not corrects a defect). E1_W1_L2 — Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design Talking Point: In the first lecture, I introduced the AMVF and showed you where measurement sits in the framework — on the physical modeling branch. Now I want to go deeper into what measurement actually is. Not how to press buttons on a sensor, but what a sensor is really doing: what it reports, what it infers, and what it silently assumes. And in the second half of this lecture, I'll introduce the formal vocabulary of experiment design — the language you will use in your Phase 1 design deliverable this week when you commit to a hypothesis and a procedure for your assigned canned experiment before any data collection begins. ================================================================================ slide 2: Measurement Is Model Realization (Image: A two-layer diagram on a clean white background. Top layer labeled "Physical Reality" shows a schematic of a physical system — a rotating wheel with angular velocity arrows, labeled "True Angular Velocity: ω." Bottom layer labeled "Sensor Output" shows a voltage-vs-time trace with visible noise and a slight baseline offset, labeled "Recorded Signal: 2.34 V ± noise." Between the two layers, a downward arrow passes through a gray box labeled with four stages: Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Analog-to-Digital Conversion → Calibration Equation. A red annotation at the bottom reads "This is not the physical quantity — it is a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this lecture, and I want it to land early: measurement is not data collection. Measurement is model realization. When you place a sensor on a structure or a system, you are not directly recording a physical truth — you are producing an inference about the physical world through a chain of transduction, conditioning, digitization, and calibration assumptions. Every step in that chain introduces error. Every step reflects a modeling choice. The voltage you read on your screen is not the angular velocity or the force or the temperature — it is the output of a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what your measurement is actually telling you. The physical modeling branch of the AMVF starts here — in this chain. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Transducer Chain (Image: A horizontal five-box chain diagram on a light background, boxes connected by right-pointing arrows. Box 1: "Physical Phenomenon" — icon of a force arrow on a surface. Box 2: "Transducer Element" — icon of a sensor element converting mechanical energy to electrical, labeled "e.g., strain gauge → resistance change." Box 3: "Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform being discretized into stepped digital levels, with arrow labeled "sampling rate matters here." Box 5: "Recorded Value" — a number in a spreadsheet cell. A red callout below Box 2 reads "Each stage introduces its own error source." A separate callout at Box 4 reads "Sampling and aliasing: Phase II, Week 7." The chain runs left to right across the full width of the slide.) Talking Point: The transducer chain is the sequence of physical and electronic processes that converts a real-world quantity into a recorded number. It starts at the phenomenon you care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in between is a transformation, and every transformation can introduce error. The transducer converts energy from one form to another: a piezoelectric crystal produces voltage in response to strain, a thermocouple generates voltage proportional to temperature difference, a MEMS accelerometer changes capacitance in response to inertial force. Signal conditioning amplifies, filters, and excites. The analog-to-digital converter discretizes the continuous signal. I will not go deep into sampling theory today — that is Week 7 in Phase II. But I want you to see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause. ================================================================================ slide 4: What We Measure vs. What We Infer (Image: A two-column diagram. Left column header: "What the Sensor Reports" — a blue box showing "Voltage: 2.34 V" with a voltmeter icon. Right column header: "What We Care About" — a gold box showing "Force: 14.7 ± 0.4 N" with a force arrow icon. Between the columns, a downward-pointing conversion block contains: "Calibration Equation: F = k·V + b" and below it "Assumptions: linear response, stable calibration constants, constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the assumptions in the conversion." An upward arrow from the right column back to the left with a dashed line asks: "If one assumption breaks — which column is wrong?") Talking Point: Sensors almost never directly measure the quantity you care about. A strain gauge measures resistance change. A thermocouple measures voltage. A pressure transducer measures membrane deflection. What you actually want is strain, temperature, or pressure. The conversion from what you measure to what you infer is not automatic — it is a calibration equation built on physical assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the electrical excitation fluctuates — the inference breaks too, even though the raw voltage reading is perfectly accurate. This is why calibration is not optional, and it is why I will ask you in every lab report to state explicitly what your sensor measured versus what you inferred from that measurement. The distinction is the beginning of experimental honesty. ================================================================================ slide 5: Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies (Image: A four-quadrant target diagram. Each quadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster shifted far from center, labeled "consistent but wrong." Top-right: "Low Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster near center, labeled "ideal." Bottom-left: "High Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster far from center, labeled "wrong and inconsistent." Bottom-right: "Low Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster near center, labeled "inconsistent but not systematically wrong." Below each quadrant: the remedy — "Calibration" (top-left), "No action needed" (top-right), "Calibration + more measurements" (bottom-left), "More measurements + isolation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.) Talking Point: Bias and precision are two distinct properties of a measurement system, and they require completely different interventions. Bias is a systematic offset — every reading is shifted by a consistent amount in the same direction. A poorly zeroed load cell, a thermocouple with a bad reference junction, a sensor mounted with mechanical preload — these all introduce bias. Bias can sometimes be corrected by calibration against a known reference, but only if you have that reference. Precision is about scatter — how much do repeated measurements of the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all biased. Low precision means your measurements scatter widely. Knowing which problem you have determines the solution: bias problems call for better calibration; precision problems call for more repetition, better isolation, and better experimental control. You will characterize both in your Phase 1 experiment. ================================================================================ slide 6: Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source (Image: A cross-sectional engineering sketch of a sensor mounted in a physical system. Three annotation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point, thermocouple reads lower than true fluid temperature." (2) "Mounting stress — over-tightening changes the mechanical boundary condition, altering strain at the gauge location." (3) "Electromagnetic interference — signal wire runs near a motor power lead, inducing 60 Hz noise into the measurement channel." The sketch is schematic and black-and-white with red callout arrows. A text note at the bottom: "Installation errors often exceed the sensor specification error. They are design choices, not random events.") Talking Point: A sensor that has been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field. Installation effects are the category of errors introduced by how and where a sensor is placed in the physical environment, and they are chronically underestimated by students and sometimes by professionals. Conduction error occurs when the sensor body conducts thermal energy away from the measurement point, pulling the reading toward ambient temperature. Mounting stress changes the mechanical state at the sensor location — a classic problem with strain gauges on thin or flexible structures. Electromagnetic interference is a signal contamination problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in aerospace laboratory work, and your experimental design this week must anticipate them before you take the first reading. If you discover an installation effect after the data is collected, you have a confound — not a measurement. ================================================================================ slide 7: Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A calibration curve graph on a clean white background. X-axis labeled "Known Reference Input (True Value)" with regular tick marks. Y-axis labeled "Sensor Output (Raw Units)." Two curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual Measured Response." The region between the curves is highlighted in yellow and labeled "Calibration Residual." Below the graph, a best-fit equation: "Output = a·Input + b, R² = 0.9992." In the bottom-right corner, a red text box: "Calibration adjusts the sensor output to match a known reference. It corrects bias. It does NOT validate a model.") Talking Point: Calibration is the process of comparing a sensor's output to a known reference standard and using that comparison to derive a characterization equation — a scale factor, an offset, a polynomial fit — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If your force sensor reads five percent high across its full range, calibration corrects that. Calibration is a measurement activity — it improves the fidelity of what the sensor reports about the physical world. I want to be explicit about what calibration is not: it is not validation. Calibration tells you the sensor is correctly reporting the physical quantity. Validation tells you whether your model accurately predicts system behavior. These are different claims about different things, and we will develop this distinction carefully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model. ================================================================================ slide 8: Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference (Image: A fishbone cause-and-effect diagram. The central horizontal spine points to a box labeled "Measured Outcome." Four angled branches extend from the spine, each labeled: "Environment" (sub-causes: temperature drift, humidity, ambient vibration), "Instrumentation" (sub-causes: sensor drift, noise floor, calibration age), "Operator" (sub-causes: positioning variability, recording inconsistency, timing errors), "Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch is clean and labeled in a consistent font. A red callout at the center node reads "Confounders produce apparent effects that are not caused by the independent variable.") Talking Point: A confounding factor is anything that causes your dependent variable to change for reasons other than the independent variable you're manipulating. If you're measuring the period of a pendulum while someone opens a window, and the airflow changes the drag on the pendulum bob, that's a confound. If you're measuring structural deflection and the temperature in the lab changes between trials, altering the material modulus, that's a confound. Confounders are dangerous because they can produce real, repeatable, statistically consistent apparent effects that have nothing to do with the phenomenon you are studying. The antidote to confounding is experimental design: controlled conditions, documented environment, randomized trial order, and careful isolation of the variable you are trying to manipulate. One of the required elements of your Phase 1 design deliverable this week is to identify at least one confounding factor in your assigned experiment before you run it — because after you have collected the data is the wrong time to remember them. ================================================================================ slide 9: Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary (Image: A structured three-section diagram on a clean white background, each section a labeled card. Card 1: "Hypothesis" — template text: "If [independent variable] is changed by [specific amount or direction], then [dependent variable] will [specific expected response], because [physical reasoning in one sentence]." Card 2: "Variables" — a three-column table with column headers: Independent Variable (what you change), Dependent Variable (what you measure), Controlled Variables (what you hold fixed). Card 3: "Confounders" — a short bullet list labeled "Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented." Below all three cards: a note reading "All three elements are required in the Phase 1 design deliverable this week." The cards are visually distinct with subtle color coding.) Talking Point: Formal experiment design requires specific vocabulary, and I want to give you that vocabulary precisely. A hypothesis is a specific, falsifiable, causal statement — it is not a question, it is not a guess, and it is not a vague expectation. An independent variable is the quantity you intentionally change. A dependent variable is the quantity you measure in response. Controlled variables are the quantities you hold constant so they do not contaminate the relationship you are studying. Together these three elements define the scope of the experiment — they tell you and everyone who reads your report what you were actually testing. This week's Phase 1 design deliverable requires all three, along with a hypothesis and at least one identified confounder. Come to the lab session this week ready to write these down for your assigned experiment, because that design work is what drives everything that follows. ================================================================================ slide 10: Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X: "If we change the input voltage to the circuit, the measured power will change." Below, three red annotations: "Direction unspecified," "No physical reasoning," "Not falsifiable in a useful sense." Right box, labeled "Strong Hypothesis" with a green checkmark: "If input voltage is increased from 3V to 9V while resistance is held constant, measured electrical power will increase by a factor of nine, because power is proportional to voltage squared (P = V²/R) for a fixed resistance." Below, three green annotations: "Specific quantitative prediction," "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual comparison.) Talking Point: The quality of your hypothesis determines the quality of your experiment. Let me show you exactly what I mean. The weak hypothesis — "if we change the input voltage, the power will change" — is technically true but scientifically useless. It predicts a directional change without specifying how much, and it gives no physical reasoning. If the power changes by five percent or by nine hundred percent, both confirm the weak hypothesis. The strong hypothesis specifies a quantitative prediction, states the physical mechanism, and is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of three-fold instead of nine-fold, I have a discrepancy I need to explain — and explaining it will teach me something. The strong hypothesis is the one worth testing. In your experiment design this week, your hypothesis must commit to a mechanism, not just a direction. ================================================================================ slide 11: Connecting Your Design to the AMVF (Image: The AMVF flowchart from Lecture 1, now with five sticky-note style annotations attached to specific nodes. At "Physical Realization": "Your hypothesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and calibration procedure." At "Data Reduction": "Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation." At "Experimental Outcomes": "Your measured values with uncertainty stated." At "Quantitative Comparison": "Your validation metric applied to outcomes vs. model." The rest of the diagram is lightly grayed. A label below the figure reads "Phase 1 design deliverable: annotate this diagram with your actual decisions.") Talking Point: Before you leave this lecture, I want to draw the explicit connection between everything we just discussed and the AMVF from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection, installation decisions, and calibration plan map to the instrumentation node. Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation map to data reduction. Your final reported values with uncertainty map to experimental outcomes. And your comparison to the predictive model maps to the quantitative comparison node. The Phase 1 design deliverable this week asks you to annotate an AMVF diagram with your actual design decisions at each node. That exercise is not bureaucratic — it is the most direct way to check whether every measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Is This Hypothesis Testable? (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's submitted hypothesis: "The motor will produce more torque at higher input voltage." Below the quote, a single prompt in bold: "What makes this hypothesis not testable as stated? How would you fix it to make it defensible?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this hypothesis. [Pause — allow student responses.] The claim is technically true but scientifically useless: "more torque" could mean 0.001% more or 500% more — both would confirm it. There is no quantitative prediction, no stated physical mechanism, and no threshold that a measurement could clearly contradict. A defensible version commits to all three: "If input voltage is increased from 12V to 24V while load resistance is held constant, motor output torque will increase by a factor of four, because in a DC motor under constant winding resistance and no magnetic saturation, torque scales as the square of the applied voltage." That version names the mechanism, commits to a specific ratio, and can be clearly contradicted by a measurement showing a factor of 1.8 or a factor of 10. Notice: the strong hypothesis taught you something even before the experiment ran — you had to know the physics to write it. That is the point. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure You now have two foundational layers for this course: the AMVF gives you the structural map, and measurement fundamentals give you the language for what happens at every node on that map. Measurement is not passive data collection — it is an active chain of physical and electronic modeling choices that each carry their own error. Designing an experiment means anticipating that chain before you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are inferring, and naming the confounders before they corrupt your result. This week's design deliverable is intentionally simple — the discipline of doing it carefully is not. The habits you build this week — hypothesis-first, confounder-aware, AMVF-connected — are the habits of a test engineer, and this is where they begin. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - References "assigned canned experiment" and "Phase 1 design deliverable" per Iteration 5 terminology — no "pendulum" as the specific experiment example. - P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example, connecting naturally to the W2 Lecture 2 Lab 1 activity without introducing it early. - Calibration vs. validation distinction introduced lightly in Slide 7 — tagged as "Week 3" for the deeper treatment, per blueprint continuity. - Slide 11 explicitly connects to the annotated AMVF deliverable from the W1 lab session. - Confounder identification requirement in Slide 8 and 9 matches the Phase 1 required elements listed in the W1 lab activity blueprint. - Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). The antidote to confounding is experimental design: controlled conditions, documented environment, randomized trial order, and careful isolation of the variable you are trying to manipulate. One of the required elements of your Phase 1 design deliverable this week is to identify at least one confounding factor in your assigned experiment before you run it — because after you have collected the data is the wrong time to remember them. ================================================================================ slide 9: Formal Experiment

member them. ================================================================================ slide 9: Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary (Image: A structured three-section diagram on a clean white background, each section a labeled card. Card 1: "Hypothesis" — template text: "If [independent variable] is changed by [specific amount or direction], then [dependent variable] will [specific expected response], because [physical reasoning in one sentence]." Card 2: "Variables" — a three-column table with column headers: Independent Variable (what you change), Dependent Variable (what you measure), Controlled Variables (what you hold fixed). Card 3: "Confounders" — a short bullet list labeled "Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented." Below all three cards: a note

a short bullet list labeled "Known confounders and how they are controlled or documented." Below all three cards: a note reading "All three elements are required in the Phase 1 design deliverable this week." The cards are visually distinct with subtle color coding.) Talking Point: Formal experiment design requires specific vocabulary, and I want to give you that vocabulary precisely. A hypothesis is a specific, falsifiable, causal statement — it is not a question, it is not a guess, and it is not a vague expectation. An independent variable is the quantity you intentionally change. A dependent variable is the quantity you measure in response. Controlled variables are the quantities you hold constant so they do not contaminate the relationship you are studying. Together these three

are the quantities you hold constant so they do not contaminate the relationship you are studying. Together these three elements define the scope of the experiment — they tell you and everyone who reads your report what you were actually testing. This week's Phase 1 design deliverable requires all three, along with a hypothesis and at least one identified confounder. Come to the lab session this week ready to write these down for your assigned experiment, because that design work is what drives everything that follows. ================================================================================ slide 10: Hypothesis Quality — A Direct Comparison (Image: A side-by-side comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X:

ide comparison of two hypothesis statements in clearly bordered boxes. Left box, labeled "Weak Hypothesis" with a red X: "If we change the input voltage to the circuit, the measured power will change." Below, three red annotations: "Direction unspecified," "No physical reasoning," "Not falsifiable in a useful sense." Right box, labeled "Strong Hypothesis" with a green checkmark: "If input voltage is increased from 3V to 9V while resistance is held constant, measured electrical power will increase by a factor of nine, because power is proportional to voltage squared (P = V²/R) for a fixed resistance." Below, three green annotations: "Specific quantitative prediction," "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual

" "Physical mechanism stated," "Clearly falsifiable." The two boxes are the same size and side by side for direct visual comparison.) Talking Point: The quality of your hypothesis determines the quality of your experiment. Let me show you exactly what I mean. The weak hypothesis — "if we change the input voltage, the power will change" — is technically true but scientifically useless. It predicts a directional change without specifying how much, and it gives no physical reasoning. If the power changes by five percent or by nine hundred percent, both confirm the weak hypothesis. The strong hypothesis specifies a quantitative prediction, states the physical mechanism, and is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of

nd is falsifiable in the sense that a specific measurement could clearly contradict it. If I measure a power increase of three-fold instead of nine-fold, I have a discrepancy I need to explain — and explaining it will teach me something. The strong hypothesis is the one worth testing. In your experiment design this week, your hypothesis must commit to a mechanism, not just a direction. ================================================================================ slide 11: Connecting Your Design to the AMVF (Image: The AMVF flowchart from Lecture 1, now with five sticky-note style annotations attached to specific nodes. At "Physical Realization": "Your hypothesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and

hesis, procedure, and experimental setup." At "Experimental Measurement": "Your sensor selection, installation plan, and calibration procedure." At "Data Reduction": "Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation." At "Experimental Outcomes": "Your measured values with uncertainty stated." At "Quantitative Comparison": "Your validation metric applied to outcomes vs. model." The rest of the diagram is lightly grayed. A label below the figure reads "Phase 1 design deliverable: annotate this diagram with your actual decisions.") Talking Point: Before you leave this lecture, I want to draw the explicit connection between everything we just discussed and the AMVF from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection,

from Lecture 1. Your hypothesis and experimental procedure map to the physical realization node. Your sensor selection, installation decisions, and calibration plan map to the instrumentation node. Your MATLAB analysis and uncertainty propagation map to data reduction. Your final reported values with uncertainty map to experimental outcomes. And your comparison to the predictive model maps to the quantitative comparison node. The Phase 1 design deliverable this week asks you to annotate an AMVF diagram with your actual design decisions at each node. That exercise is not bureaucratic — it is the most direct way to check whether every measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate.

measurement decision you are making has a purpose in the framework and connects to the model you are trying to evaluate. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Is This Hypothesis Testable? (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's submitted hypothesis: "The motor will produce more torque at higher input voltage." Below the quote, a single prompt in bold: "What makes this hypothesis not testable as stated? How would you fix it to make it defensible?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this hypothesis. [Pause — allow student responses.] The claim is technically true but scientifically useless: "more torque" could mean 0.001% more or 500% more — both would confirm it. There is no

true but scientifically useless: "more torque" could mean 0.001% more or 500% more — both would confirm it. There is no quantitative prediction, no stated physical mechanism, and no threshold that a measurement could clearly contradict. A defensible version commits to all three: "If input voltage is increased from 12V to 24V while load resistance is held constant, motor output torque will increase by a factor of four, because in a DC motor under constant winding resistance and no magnetic saturation, torque scales as the square of the applied voltage." That version names the mechanism, commits to a specific ratio, and can be clearly contradicted by a measurement showing a factor of 1.8 or a factor of 10. Notice: the strong hypothesis taught you something even before the experiment ran —

g a factor of 1.8 or a factor of 10. Notice: the strong hypothesis taught you something even before the experiment ran — you had to know the physics to write it. That is the point. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure You now have two foundational layers for this course: the AMVF gives you the structural map, and measurement fundamentals give you the language for what happens at every node on that map. Measurement is not passive data collection — it is an active chain of physical and electronic modeling choices that each carry their own error. Designing an experiment means anticipating that chain before you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are

ore you run it: writing a hypothesis that commits to a mechanism, identifying what you are measuring versus what you are inferring, and naming the confounders before they corrupt your result. This week's design deliverable is intentionally simple — the discipline of doing it carefully is not. The habits you build this week — hypothesis-first, confounder-aware, AMVF-connected — are the habits of a test engineer, and this is where they begin. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example, connecting

n 5 terminology — no "pendulum" as the specific experiment example. P=IV used in Slide 10 hypothesis example, connecting naturally to the W2 Lecture 2 Lab 1 activity without introducing it early. Confounder identification requirement in Slide 8 and 9 matches the Phase 1 required elements listed in the W1 lab activity blueprint. luator Moment — hypothesis falsifiability).
