E1_W1_L2 — Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 1, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Measurement Fundamentals & Formal Experiment Design Talking Point: In the first lecture, I introduced the AMVF and showed you where measurement sits in the framework — on the physical modeling branch. Now I want to go

uced the AMVF and showed you where measurement sits in the framework — on the physical modeling branch. Now I want to go deeper into what measurement actually is. Not how to press buttons on a sensor, but what a sensor is really doing: what it reports, what it infers, and what it silently assumes. And in the second half of this lecture, I'll introduce the formal vocabulary of experiment design — the language you will use in your Phase 1 design deliverable this week when you commit to a hypothesis and a procedure for your assigned canned experiment before any data collection begins. ================================================================================ slide 2: Measurement Is Model Realization (Image: A two-layer diagram on a clean white background. Top layer labeled "Physical

2: Measurement Is Model Realization (Image: A two-layer diagram on a clean white background. Top layer labeled "Physical Reality" shows a schematic of a physical system — a rotating wheel with angular velocity arrows, labeled "True Angular Velocity: ω." Bottom layer labeled "Sensor Output" shows a voltage-vs-time trace with visible noise and a slight baseline offset, labeled "Recorded Signal: 2.34 V ± noise." Between the two layers, a downward arrow passes through a gray box labeled with four stages: Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Analog-to-Digital Conversion → Calibration Equation. A red annotation at the bottom reads "This is not the physical quantity — it is a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this

a model of the physical quantity realized through hardware.") Talking Point: Here is the most important concept in this lecture, and I want it to land early: measurement is not data collection. Measurement is model realization. When you place a sensor on a structure or a system, you are not directly recording a physical truth — you are producing an inference about the physical world through a chain of transduction, conditioning, digitization, and calibration assumptions. Every step in that chain introduces error. Every step reflects a modeling choice. The voltage you read on your screen is not the angular velocity or the force or the temperature — it is the output of a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what

a physical model of how your sensor responds to that quantity. If you do not understand that chain, you cannot know what your measurement is actually telling you. The physical modeling branch of the AMVF starts here — in this chain. ================================================================================ slide 3: The Transducer Chain (Image: A horizontal five-box chain diagram on a light background, boxes connected by right-pointing arrows. Box 1: "Physical Phenomenon" — icon of a force arrow on a surface. Box 2: "Transducer Element" — icon of a sensor element converting mechanical energy to electrical, labeled "e.g., strain gauge → resistance change." Box 3: "Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform

"Signal Conditioning" — circuit icon labeled "amplification, filtering, excitation." Box 4: "A/D Conversion" — waveform being discretized into stepped digital levels, with arrow labeled "sampling rate matters here." Box 5: "Recorded Value" — a number in a spreadsheet cell. A red callout below Box 2 reads "Each stage introduces its own error source." A separate callout at Box 4 reads "Sampling and aliasing: Phase II, Week 7." The chain runs left to right across the full width of the slide.) Talking Point: The transducer chain is the sequence of physical and electronic processes that converts a real-world quantity into a recorded number. It starts at the phenomenon you care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in

care about — a force, a temperature, an angular velocity — and ends in a file somewhere on your computer. Every stage in between is a transformation, and every transformation can introduce error. The transducer converts energy from one form to another: a piezoelectric crystal produces voltage in response to strain, a thermocouple generates voltage proportional to temperature difference, a MEMS accelerometer changes capacitance in response to inertial force. Signal conditioning amplifies, filters, and excites. The analog-to-digital converter discretizes the continuous signal. I will not go deep into sampling theory today — that is Week 7 in Phase II. But I want you to see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause.

see this chain clearly right now, because when your measurement looks wrong, this chain is where you look for the cause. ================================================================================ slide 4: What We Measure vs. What We Infer (Image: A two-column diagram. Left column header: "What the Sensor Reports" — a blue box showing "Voltage: 2.34 V" with a voltmeter icon. Right column header: "What We Care About" — a gold box showing "Force: 14.7 ± 0.4 N" with a force arrow icon. Between the columns, a downward-pointing conversion block contains: "Calibration Equation: F = k·V + b" and below it "Assumptions: linear response, stable calibration constants, constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the

constant temperature, no mounting slip." A red annotation below the right column: "The inference is only as good as the assumptions in the conversion." An upward arrow from the right column back to the left with a dashed line asks: "If one assumption breaks — which column is wrong?") Talking Point: Sensors almost never directly measure the quantity you care about. A strain gauge measures resistance change. A thermocouple measures voltage. A pressure transducer measures membrane deflection. What you actually want is strain, temperature, or pressure. The conversion from what you measure to what you infer is not automatic — it is a calibration equation built on physical assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the

assumptions. If any of those assumptions break down — the sensor drifts with temperature, the mounting point shifts, the electrical excitation fluctuates — the inference breaks too, even though the raw voltage reading is perfectly accurate. This is why calibration is not optional, and it is why I will ask you in every lab report to state explicitly what your sensor measured versus what you inferred from that measurement. The distinction is the beginning of experimental honesty. ================================================================================ slide 5: Bias vs. Precision — Different Problems, Different Remedies (Image: A four-quadrant target diagram. Each quadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight

uadrant shows a cluster of data points relative to a bullseye at center. Top-left: "High Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster shifted far from center, labeled "consistent but wrong." Top-right: "Low Bias, High Precision" — a tight cluster near center, labeled "ideal." Bottom-left: "High Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster far from center, labeled "wrong and inconsistent." Bottom-right: "Low Bias, Low Precision" — a scattered cluster near center, labeled "inconsistent but not systematically wrong." Below each quadrant: the remedy — "Calibration" (top-left), "No action needed" (top-right), "Calibration + more measurements" (bottom-left), "More measurements + isolation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.)

lation" (bottom-right). The four quadrants are clearly bordered, with the top-right shown in gold to indicate the goal.) Talking Point: Bias and precision are two distinct properties of a measurement system, and they require completely different interventions. Bias is a systematic offset — every reading is shifted by a consistent amount in the same direction. A poorly zeroed load cell, a thermocouple with a bad reference junction, a sensor mounted with mechanical preload — these all introduce bias. Bias can sometimes be corrected by calibration against a known reference, but only if you have that reference. Precision is about scatter — how much do repeated measurements of the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all

f the same quantity disagree with each other? High precision means your measurements cluster tightly even if they're all biased. Low precision means your measurements scatter widely. Knowing which problem you have determines the solution: bias problems call for better calibration; precision problems call for more repetition, better isolation, and better experimental control. You will characterize both in your Phase 1 experiment. ================================================================================ slide 6: Installation Effects — The Silent Error Source (Image: A cross-sectional engineering sketch of a sensor mounted in a physical system. Three annotation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point,

tation arrows point to specific features: (1) "Conduction error — sensor body conducts heat away from measurement point, thermocouple reads lower than true fluid temperature." (2) "Mounting stress — over-tightening changes the mechanical boundary condition, altering strain at the gauge location." (3) "Electromagnetic interference — signal wire runs near a motor power lead, inducing 60 Hz noise into the measurement channel." The sketch is schematic and black-and-white with red callout arrows. A text note at the bottom: "Installation errors often exceed the sensor specification error. They are design choices, not random events.") Talking Point: A sensor that has been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field.

s been perfectly calibrated in a laboratory can still produce terrible data if it is installed incorrectly in the field. Installation effects are the category of errors introduced by how and where a sensor is placed in the physical environment, and they are chronically underestimated by students and sometimes by professionals. Conduction error occurs when the sensor body conducts thermal energy away from the measurement point, pulling the reading toward ambient temperature. Mounting stress changes the mechanical state at the sensor location — a classic problem with strain gauges on thin or flexible structures. Electromagnetic interference is a signal contamination problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in

problem introduced by running wires near power sources. These are not exotic failure modes. They are routine problems in aerospace laboratory work, and your experimental design this week must anticipate them before you take the first reading. If you discover an installation effect after the data is collected, you have a confound — not a measurement. ================================================================================ slide 7: Calibration Basics — What It Is and What It Is Not (Image: A calibration curve graph on a clean white background. X-axis labeled "Known Reference Input (True Value)" with regular tick marks. Y-axis labeled "Sensor Output (Raw Units)." Two curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual

wo curves shown: a dashed straight line labeled "Ideal Linear Response" and a slightly curved solid line labeled "Actual Measured Response." The region between the curves is highlighted in yellow and labeled "Calibration Residual." Below the graph, a best-fit equation: "Output = a·Input + b, R² = 0.9992." In the bottom-right corner, a red text box: "Calibration adjusts the sensor output to match a known reference. It corrects bias. It does NOT validate a model.") Talking Point: Calibration is the process of comparing a sensor's output to a known reference standard and using that comparison to derive a characterization equation — a scale factor, an offset, a polynomial fit — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If

t — that describes how the sensor's output maps to the true physical quantity. It removes or reduces systematic bias. If your force sensor reads five percent high across its full range, calibration corrects that. Calibration is a measurement activity — it improves the fidelity of what the sensor reports about the physical world. I want to be explicit about what calibration is not: it is not validation. Calibration tells you the sensor is correctly reporting the physical quantity. Validation tells you whether your model accurately predicts system behavior. These are different claims about different things, and we will develop this distinction carefully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model.

efully in Week 3. For now, just carry this: calibrating a sensor improves your measurement. It does not test your model. ================================================================================ slide 8: Confounding Factors — Threats to Causal Inference (Image: A fishbone cause-and-effect diagram. The central horizontal spine points to a box labeled "Measured Outcome." Four angled branches extend from the spine, each labeled: "Environment" (sub-causes: temperature drift, humidity, ambient vibration), "Instrumentation" (sub-causes: sensor drift, noise floor, calibration age), "Operator" (sub-causes: positioning variability, recording inconsistency, timing errors), "Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch

"Experimental Setup" (sub-causes: boundary condition variation, fixture compliance, thermal equilibration). Each branch is clean and labeled in a consistent font. A red callout at the center node reads "Confounders produce apparent effects that are not caused by the independent variable.") If you're measuring structural deflection and the temperature in the lab changes between trials, altering the material modulus, that's a confound. Confounders are dangerous because they can

lab changes between trials, altering the material modulus, that's a confound. member them. ================================================================================ slide 9: Formal Experiment Design — The Vocabulary (Image: A structured three-section diagram on a clean white background, each section a labeled card. pages.title: R01
pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Reading R01 # AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation ## ASME V&V 10-2006, Measurement Uncertainty Standards, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework Every claim an engineer makes about... AMVF Reference: Engineering Standards and the Professional Vocabulary of Validation ASME V&V 10-2006, Measurement Uncertainty Standards, and the Handbooks Behind the Framework Every claim an engineer makes about a system is only as credible as the framework they used to make it — and professional experimentalists have spent decades building that framework already. The Aerospace Modeling & Validation Framework introduced in Week 1's lecture is not an invention of this course. It is a synthesis of engineering practice that has been codified over decades in standards documents, measurement handbooks, and validation guides produced by professional organizations whose job is to decide how engineers should argue for results. This reading is a map to those resources — not a reading list you must complete now, but a set of pointers that will become required references as the course progresses. The most important of these documents is ASME V&V 10-2006 , the primary engineering standard anchoring the AMVF framework used throughout ASEN 3501. Published by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, V&V 10-2006 establishes the conceptual and procedural framework for verification and validation in computational engineering. Its full title — Guide for Verification and Validation in Computational Fluid Dynamics and Heat Transfer — signals that its procedural requirements are specific to computational modeling; its conceptual vocabulary, however — verification, validation, model adequacy, uncertainty — has been widely adopted as the standard framework language across both computational and experimental engineering, and it is in that conceptual role that this course uses it. It defines those terms — verification, validation, uncertainty, model adequacy — in the precise, technical sense that practicing engineers use. When the Week 1 lecture distinguished verification (did you solve the equations correctly?) from validation (are your equations the right ones for this problem?), those definitions trace directly to this document. When the AMVF flowchart separates mathematical modeling from physical modeling and places quantitative comparison at the intersection, that architecture reflects the V&V 10-2006 framework. This standard is the language primer for everything that follows in this course. <image description: A two-column diagram showing the V&V framework structure. On the left, a "Mathematical Model" branch descends through equation derivation and numerical solution, terminating at "Verification." On the right, a "Physical Model" branch descends through experiment design, data collection, and uncertainty quantification, terminating at "Experimental Outcomes." A horizontal bridge labeled "Validation" connects the two branches at the bottom, with a bidirectional arrow labeled "Quantitative Comparison" spanning the gap. The overall structure mirrors the AMVF flowchart. A label in the lower corner reads "ASME V&V 10-2006." Clean black-on-white engineering diagram style, minimal shading, clear label typography.> Beyond V&V 10-2006, practicing aerospace experimentalists draw on a cluster of related documents. The AIAA measurement standards — produced by the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics — address measurement methodology specific to aerospace applications: wind tunnel testing, flight measurements, and propulsion characterization. These standards specify how uncertainty should be treated in measurement-intensive aerospace environments and will surface explicitly when E2's more complex experimental setups are introduced. The key point is that measurement standards are not generic — different engineering domains have domain-specific standards that define acceptable practice, and knowing which standard applies to your experiment is part of experimental design. For uncertainty analysis specifically, the ASME PTC (Performance Test Codes) standards provide procedural requirements for measurement uncertainty in engineering performance testing. These standards require that every reported result carry explicit uncertainty bounds derived by methods consistent with the standard's prescribed framework. If you are ever asked whether your measurement result is credible in a professional or regulatory context, the answer lives in whether your uncertainty analysis conforms to a recognized standard. <image description: A layered pyramid diagram with three tiers. The top tier is labeled "Primary Standards" and shows ASME V&V 10-2006 and AIAA measurement standards as formal document icons. The middle tier is labeled "Reference Texts" and shows Coleman & Steele as a textbook icon alongside the ISO GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) as a second document icon. The bottom tier is labeled "Practitioner Tools" and shows icons for lab documentation, MATLAB, and data recording. Arrows point downward from each tier to the tier below, indicating that the upper layers define the rules that lower-layer practice must follow. Flat-design illustration style, CU Boulder gold and black color palette.> The reference that bridges all of these to classroom practice is Coleman & Steele , Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers (4th edition). This book is the accessible bridge between the formal standards and the methods you will use in this course — beginning in Week 2, when partial derivative error propagation and Monte Carlo simulation are introduced. Relevant chapters will be assigned as the course progresses; having the book as a reference from Week 1 means you can follow the lecture directly into its theoretical foundations whenever you want to go deeper. The professional implication is easy to miss on first encounter: when you apply the AMVF in a lab report, you are not following a course-specific convention — you are operating within the same framework that engineers at NASA, aerospace contractors, and national labs use when they are required to document that a test was conducted correctly. Standards documents are the mechanism by which that framework is enforced across organizations and over time. ASME V&V 10-2006 will be a required reference in E3 when you develop your own V&V plan. The AIAA and ASME PTC standards will surface in E2. Knowing they exist — and having a sense of what they contain — changes how you read your own results. The Takeaway: ASME V&V 10-2006 is the professional anchor for the framework this course builds on — not a textbook chapter, but the actual document that practicing engineers cite when arguing that a test was done right. From Week 1, you are working within that professional tradition, not just a course format.

pages.title: R02
pages.text: E1 Week 1 (Course Week 1) Reading R02 # GitHub Basics for Course Documentation ## Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened — and in professional aerospace p... GitHub Basics for Course Documentation Your Experiment Lives Longer Than Your Lab Session — Here's Where to Put It An undocumented experiment is an experiment that never happened — and in professional aerospace practice, an experiment that exists only in memory is worth nothing to the next engineer who needs to reproduce, extend, or audit your work. Engineering documentation has two jobs: it preserves what you did and why you did it, and it makes your work checkable by someone who was not there. Professional test engineers maintain meticulous records not because they enjoy paperwork, but because the value of a measurement result depends entirely on the ability of a third party to evaluate it independently. A voltage reading without context is noise. A voltage reading with a documented procedure, sensor specification, calibration record, and uncertainty estimate is evidence. GitHub is a platform for hosting and sharing documents using version control — a system that tracks every change you make to a file over time, along with who made the change and when. In software development, this is used to manage code across large teams. In this course, you will use it more simply: as a public-facing portfolio where each experiment you conduct has a documented home and a traceable history. <image description: A screenshot-style illustration of a GitHub repository page for a hypothetical student project called "ASEN3501-Portfolio." The repository shows a README.md file rendered at the top with a brief description: "ASEN 3501 experiment documentation — Weeks 1–15." Below the README, a file tree shows folders labeled "E1_week_1/", "E1_week_2/", and files including "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." The interface uses GitHub's actual visual style — white background, dark top navigation bar, file tree with icons — rendered as a clean flat illustration rather than a screenshot. A small commit message is visible below the file tree: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 experimental design and AMVF diagram.""> The mechanics are straightforward. A repository (repo) is a folder with version history — a project folder that remembers every saved version. A README.md is a text file written in Markdown — a lightweight formatting language — that appears on the repository's front page and describes what the project is. You will write your experiment documentation in Markdown: plain text with simple syntax for headers ( # Title ), bold ( **term** ), and lists ( - item ) that renders cleanly in the browser. No special software required beyond a text editor and a browser. The workflow for adding files has three steps: stage (select which files to include in this snapshot), commit (save a labeled snapshot with a short note about what changed and why), and push (send that snapshot to GitHub's servers so it is accessible online). For this course you will not need the full depth of version control — branches, pull requests, merge conflicts — but understanding the commit as "a labeled save with a note about what changed" is the fundamental concept. Your commit messages are part of your documentation narrative; write them as though you are explaining your lab session to a colleague six months from now. <image description: A three-step horizontal workflow diagram. Step 1 is labeled "Stage" and shows a folder icon with two files highlighted — "hypothesis.md" and "AMVF_annotation.md." Step 2 is labeled "Commit" with a text bubble showing the message: "Add Tier 1 Phase 1 design: hypothesis, variables, AMVF diagram." Step 3 is labeled "Push" with an upward arrow pointing to a cloud icon labeled "GitHub repository." Arrows connect each step left to right. Flat-design icons, CU Boulder gold accent color on the step labels, black and white otherwise.> Getting started requires creating a free account at github.com, creating a new repository for ASEN 3501, and uploading your first file — which will be the pre-lab design work from this week's Tier 1 Phase 1 design session. That first file should include your experimental hypothesis, your identified variables, and a description of your annotated AMVF diagram. The repository structure you establish now will carry forward through all subsequent experiment deliverables. Use AI tools freely to help with syntax questions, Markdown formatting, or workflow steps — this is exactly the kind of mechanical task where AI augmentation is efficient and appropriate. One habit worth building from the start: GitHub is a record of your process, not a drop box for final products. A repository with one massive commit at the end of the semester tells no story. Commits made as work progresses — small, frequent, with clear messages — create a record that shows how your thinking developed. That process record is a professional artifact. Engineers and employers who look at your GitHub portfolio are reading your technical judgment over time, not just the last thing you submitted. The Takeaway: The Tier 1 Phase 1 pre-lab work from this week's design session is your first portfolio entry — commit it to your repository with a clear description, and begin the documentation habit that every subsequent lab deliverable will extend.

pages.title: L01
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Lecture L01 E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ========================================================... E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty Talking Point: I want to open this lecture with a quote from Carl Richards — a financial advisor, not an engineer, but someone who understood something deeply true about working with imperfect information: "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything." That sentence captures the epistemic situation of every experimentalist. You designed a careful procedure. You installed your sensors correctly. You checked your calibration. And your data still varies. What is that variation? Is it your instrument? Is it the physical system? Is it noise? Is it a problem — or is it information? This lecture gives you the vocabulary to answer those questions precisely, and the answers determine every claim you will ever make from experimental data. ================================================================================ slide 2: Three Things That Are Not the Same (Image: A clean three-circle diagram arranged in a downward triangle, with no overlaps — each circle is clearly separate. Top-left circle labeled "Variability" — gold color — with a sub-note below: "A property of the physical world. Real, not an artifact." Top-right circle labeled "Measurement Error" — blue color — with sub-note: "A property of the instrument and process. Introduced by measurement, not by the phenomenon." Bottom circle labeled "Uncertainty" — gray with gold border — with sub-note: "A quantified epistemic claim. Describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world." Two small arrows from the top circles point down toward the bottom circle with the label "Both contribute to." A caption below the diagram: "These are distinct. Using them interchangeably produces wrong conclusions.") Talking Point: I want to establish the three-way distinction that underpins every uncertainty discussion in this course. Variability is a property of the physical world — the system itself behaves slightly differently from trial to trial, and that is real, not an artifact of your instrument. Measurement error is a property of your instrument and process — noise in the signal chain, inconsistent operator technique, thermal drift in electronics. Error is introduced by the act of measuring. Uncertainty is different from both: it is a quantified statement of how much you do not know about the true value of the quantity you are estimating. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester. ================================================================================ slide 3: Variability — Real Physics, Not Instrument Noise (Image: A time-series scatter plot showing repeated measurements of the same physical quantity over 30 consecutive trials, plotted as individual dots connected by a line. Values fluctuate around a mean of approximately 1.42 with a visible spread of ±0.04 units. A horizontal dashed line marks the mean. An annotation box on the right: "This scatter is not instrument noise — it is real physical variation in the system." A secondary panel below shows two overlapping Gaussian distributions side by side: one narrow one labeled "Instrument Noise Floor" and one wider one labeled "Physical Variability." The wider distribution clearly dominates. Caption: "In this case, eliminating instrument noise would not eliminate scatter.") Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. The mistake I see most often in lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Sometimes the scatter is telling you about the physics, and recognizing that is actually more valuable than achieving a tight instrument spec. Know the difference before you draw conclusions. ================================================================================ slide 4: Measurement Error — Systematic and Random (Image: Two side-by-side panels. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of measurement values symmetric around the true value dashed line, with a broad bell shape. Annotation: "Zero-mean fluctuation. Averages toward zero. Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.) Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected. ================================================================================ slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim (Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. The bar is labeled "Best Estimate: 14.7 N" with a two-headed vertical arrow spanning ±0.4 N, labeled "Uncertainty Interval." To the right: a formal statement box reading "Result: 14.7 ± 0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Below the bar: a contrast pair. Left: a bare number "14.7 N" with a red label "Not an engineering claim — cannot be used for comparison." Right: the same number with the uncertainty statement, with a green label "Engineering claim — can be compared to a model prediction." A definition box at the bottom: "Uncertainty: a quantified bound on how large the error could plausibly be, expressed as a probability interval.") Talking Point: Uncertainty is the number that converts a raw measurement into an engineering claim. Here is the key insight: you almost never know the actual error in your measurement. If you knew the error exactly, you would have corrected for it. What you can do is bound the plausible magnitude of the error, and express that bound as a probability interval. When I say the force is 14.7 plus or minus 0.4 Newtons at 95% confidence, I am saying that based on my knowledge of my instrument, my calibration procedure, and my measurement process, I am 95% confident the true value lies within 0.4 N of 14.7 N. That is an engineering claim. The bare number 14.7 N, without the interval, is just an observation — it cannot be meaningfully compared to a model prediction, because I cannot determine whether agreement is real or coincidental. Every result in this course must carry its uncertainty, stated explicitly with a confidence level and a method. ASME PTC 19.1 and professional measurement standards require this — it is not a course-specific convention but the professional standard for experimental uncertainty reporting, separate from and complementary to the modeling framework of ASME V&V 10-2006. ================================================================================ slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions (Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.) Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why. One practical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the uncertainty in estimating the mean from limited data than the Gaussian approximation; MATLAB's tinv function gives you the correct coverage factor for any sample size. ================================================================================ slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean (Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. A caption below: "A 95% CI means: if we repeated this experiment many times with this same N, 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true value." A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.") Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards. ================================================================================ slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result. ================================================================================ slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.") Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks. ================================================================================ slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows (Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.) Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — What Is Missing from This Conclusion? (Image: A bar graph showing three side-by-side bars on a white background. Blue bars labeled "Measured Power" at three voltage levels (3V, 6V, 9V) with values approximately 1.8 W, 7.1 W, 16.4 W. Orange bars labeled "Model Prediction (P = V²/R)" at the same voltage levels. The bars appear close in height. No error bars appear on any bar. Below the figure: a caption reading "Measured power agrees well with the model prediction across all three voltage conditions." A bold prompt below: "Is this conclusion defensible? What is missing?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this figure and caption. [Pause — allow student responses.] The conclusion is not defensible. Without uncertainty bounds on the bars, you cannot determine whether the visual agreement is meaningful or coincidental. The gap between each blue and orange bar might exceed the measurement uncertainty — in which case you have a discrepancy requiring explanation. Or the gap might be negligible — in which case agreement is supported. But you cannot tell which from this figure. The word "agrees" requires a numerical comparison within stated uncertainty bounds, applied using a pre-committed metric. Without uncertainty intervals on every bar, the conclusion has no engineering basis. A professional report that made this claim would be returned immediately for revision. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). E1_W2_L1 — Variability, Error, and Uncertainty ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty Talking Point: I want to open this lecture with a quote from Carl Richards — a financial advisor, not an engineer, but someone who understood something deeply true about working with imperfect information: "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything." That sentence captures the epistemic situation of every experimentalist. You designed a careful procedure. You installed your sensors correctly. You checked your calibration. And your data still varies. What is that variation? Is it your instrument? Is it the physical system? Is it noise? Is it a problem — or is it information? This lecture gives you the vocabulary to answer those questions precisely, and the answers determine every claim you will ever make from experimental data. ================================================================================ slide 2: Three Things That Are Not the Same (Image: A clean three-circle diagram arranged in a downward triangle, with no overlaps — each circle is clearly separate. Top-left circle labeled "Variability" — gold color — with a sub-note below: "A property of the physical world. Real, not an artifact." Top-right circle labeled "Measurement Error" — blue color — with sub-note: "A property of the instrument and process. Introduced by measurement, not by the phenomenon." Bottom circle labeled "Uncertainty" — gray with gold border — with sub-note: "A quantified epistemic claim. Describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world." Two small arrows from the top circles point down toward the bottom circle with the label "Both contribute to." A caption below the diagram: "These are distinct. Using them interchangeably produces wrong conclusions.") Talking Point: I want to establish the three-way distinction that underpins every uncertainty discussion in this course. Variability is a property of the physical world — the system itself behaves slightly differently from trial to trial, and that is real, not an artifact of your instrument. Measurement error is a property of your instrument and process — noise in the signal chain, inconsistent operator technique, thermal drift in electronics. Error is introduced by the act of measuring. Uncertainty is different from both: it is a quantified statement of how much you do not know about the true value of the quantity you are estimating. Uncertainty encompasses both variability and error, but it is an epistemic claim — it describes the state of your knowledge, not the state of the world. These three words are not interchangeable in professional engineering communication, and I will hold you to the distinction in every deliverable this semester. ================================================================================ slide 3: Variability — Real Physics, Not Instrument Noise (Image: A time-series scatter plot showing repeated measurements of the same physical quantity over 30 consecutive trials, plotted as individual dots connected by a line. Values fluctuate around a mean of approximately 1.42 with a visible spread of ±0.04 units. A horizontal dashed line marks the mean. An annotation box on the right: "This scatter is not instrument noise — it is real physical variation in the system." A secondary panel below shows two overlapping Gaussian distributions side by side: one narrow one labeled "Instrument Noise Floor" and one wider one labeled "Physical Variability." The wider distribution clearly dominates. Caption: "In this case, eliminating instrument noise would not eliminate scatter.") Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. The mistake I see most often in lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Sometimes the scatter is telling you about the physics, and recognizing that is actually more valuable than achieving a tight instrument spec. Know the difference before you draw conclusions. ================================================================================ slide 4: Measurement Error — Systematic and Random (Image: Two side-by-side panels. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of measurement values symmetric around the true value dashed line, with a broad bell shape. Annotation: "Zero-mean fluctuation. Averages toward zero. Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.) Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected. ================================================================================ slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim (Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. The bar is labeled "Best Estimate: 14.7 N" with a two-headed vertical arrow spanning ±0.4 N, labeled "Uncertainty Interval." To the right: a formal statement box reading "Result: 14.7 ± 0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Below the bar: a contrast pair. Left: a bare number "14.7 N" with a red label "Not an engineering claim — cannot be used for comparison." Right: the same number with the uncertainty statement, with a green label "Engineering claim — can be compared to a model prediction." A definition box at the bottom: "Uncertainty: a quantified bound on how large the error could plausibly be, expressed as a probability interval.") Talking Point: Uncertainty is the number that converts a raw measurement into an engineering claim. Here is the key insight: you almost never know the actual error in your measurement. If you knew the error exactly, you would have corrected for it. What you can do is bound the plausible magnitude of the error, and express that bound as a probability interval. When I say the force is 14.7 plus or minus 0.4 Newtons at 95% confidence, I am saying that based on my knowledge of my instrument, my calibration procedure, and my measurement process, I am 95% confident the true value lies within 0.4 N of 14.7 N. That is an engineering claim. The bare number 14.7 N, without the interval, is just an observation — it cannot be meaningfully compared to a model prediction, because I cannot determine whether agreement is real or coincidental. Every result in this course must carry its uncertainty, stated explicitly with a confidence level and a method. ASME PTC 19.1 and professional measurement standards require this — it is not a course-specific convention but the professional standard for experimental uncertainty reporting, separate from and complementary to the modeling framework of ASME V&V 10-2006. ================================================================================ slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions (Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. X-axis labeled "Measured Value." Y-axis labeled "Probability Density." Three vertical lines: solid at the mean μ, dashed at ±1σ from the mean, and dotted at ±2σ. Shaded regions: dark gold shading covering 68% of the area within ±1σ, lighter gold shading extending to cover 95% within ±2σ. Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.) Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. Each interval is a horizontal two-headed arrow centered on the sample mean. Interval width decreases from top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. A cautionary annotation: "This interval either does or does not contain the true value — once computed, there is no probability.") Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. ment noise would not eliminate scatter.") Talking Point: Variability is the actual physical fluctuation in the thing you are measuring — and it is real even with a perfect instrument. If you measure the period of a pendulum 30 times with a precision atomic clock, you will still get slightly different values, because the pendulum itself behaves slightly differently on each swing — small differences in release amplitude, air resistance variation, thermal expansion of the bob suspension. This variability is not a problem to be eliminated; it is information about the physical system. It tells you something real about how that system behaves. n lab reports is attributing all scatter in the data to instrument noise. Left panel: "Systematic (Bias) Error" — a histogram of measurement values shifted entirely to the right of a vertical dashed line labeled "True Value." The histogram is narrow and tight — high precision, wrong center. Annotation: "Consistent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Right panel: "Random Error" — a histogram of

tent offset in one direction. Does not average away. Fixed by calibration." Reduced by taking more measurements." Below both panels: a two-by-two summary table with rows "Reduce by averaging?" and "Reduce by calibration?" — Systematic: No / Yes. Random: Yes / No. The table is clean and easy to read.) Talking Point: Measurement error comes in two flavors that require completely different responses. Systematic error — bias — is a consistent offset in one direction. If your thermocouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is

ouple always reads two degrees high because it was calibrated in a different reference environment, every measurement is shifted the same way. No amount of averaging will fix this. The mean of a thousand biased measurements is a biased mean. To remove systematic error, you need to find its source — a calibration error, an installation effect, a thermal drift — and fix it at that source. Random error, by contrast, is zero-mean fluctuation: noise that is as likely to be high as low on any given reading. Random error does average away. Take four times as many measurements and you halve the uncertainty in your mean. Recognizing which type of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected.

ype of error dominates your measurement is the first diagnostic question you should ask when your data looks unexpected. ================================================================================ slide 5: Uncertainty — The Quantified Claim (Image: A single horizontal bar showing a best estimate with symmetric uncertainty bounds. be used for comparison." Right: the same number with the uncertainty statement, with a green label "Engineering claim — can be compared to a model prediction." Talking Point: Uncertainty is the number that converts a raw measurement into an engineering claim. Here is the key insight: you almost never know the actual error in your measurement. If you knew the error exactly, you would have corrected for it. What you can do is bound the plausible magnitude of the error, and express that bound as a probability interval. When I say the force is 14.7 plus or minus 0.4 Newtons at 95% confidence, I am saying that based on my knowledge of my

. When I say the force is 14.7 plus or minus 0.4 Newtons at 95% confidence, I am saying that based on my knowledge of my instrument, my calibration procedure, and my measurement process, I am 95% confident the true value lies within 0.4 N of 14.7 N. That is an engineering claim. The bare number 14.7 N, without the interval, is just an observation — it cannot be meaningfully compared to a model prediction, because I cannot determine whether agreement is real or coincidental. Every result in this course must carry its uncertainty, stated explicitly with a confidence level and a method. ASME PTC 19.1 and professional measurement standards require this — it is not a course-specific convention but the professional standard for experimental uncertainty reporting, separate from and complementary

pecific convention but the professional standard for experimental uncertainty reporting, separate from and complementary to the modeling framework of ASME V&V 10-2006. ================================================================================ slide 6: Statistical Properties — Mean, Standard Deviation, and Distributions (Image: A clean Gaussian bell curve on a white background. "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual

Annotations: "μ = sample mean — best estimate of the true value." "σ = standard deviation — typical spread of individual measurements." A second text box: "σ/√N = standard error of the mean — uncertainty in the mean itself, decreases with more measurements." A visual comparison of σ and σ/√N shown as two differently-sized two-headed arrows below the curve.) Talking Point: When you take repeated measurements under the same conditions, the results form a distribution. For random errors, this distribution tends toward Gaussian — the classic bell curve — as you collect more data. The mean of your measurements is your best estimate of the true value. The standard deviation tells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But

ells you how spread out individual measurements are — how much any single reading is likely to differ from the mean. But here is the distinction that trips up students: the standard deviation tells you about the scatter of individual readings, while the standard error of the mean — the standard deviation divided by the square root of N — tells you about the precision of your estimate of the mean itself. More measurements reduce the standard error of the mean. They do not reduce the physical scatter of individual measurements. When you report uncertainty on a derived quantity in this course, you need to be precise about which statistic you are using and why. One practical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the

ctical note: for small sample sizes — fewer than about 30 measurements — the t-distribution more accurately reflects the uncertainty in estimating the mean from limited data than the Gaussian approximation; MATLAB's tinv function gives you the correct coverage factor for any sample size. ================================================================================ slide 7: Confidence Intervals — What They Actually Mean (Image: A vertical stack of five horizontal confidence intervals, one per row, labeled by sample size: N = 3, N = 10, N = 30, N = 100, N = 1000. top to bottom, illustrating convergence. A vertical dashed line marks the true value. For the N=3 case, the interval is wide and the true value falls at the edge — labeled "valid, but wide." For N=1000, the interval is very narrow and centered on the true value. Talking Point: A confidence interval is one of the most consistently misunderstood constructs in data analysis, and I want to get it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true

t it right on Week 2. When I compute a 95% confidence interval, I am not saying there is a 95% probability that the true value lies within this interval. Once the interval is computed, the true value either is or is not inside it — there is no probability remaining. What 95% refers to is the procedure: if I repeated this entire experiment many times — same protocol, same number of measurements — 95% of the confidence intervals I computed would contain the true value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have

asurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards. ================================================================================ slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs.

e) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different

same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab

experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result. ================================================================================ slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty."

nch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.") Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the

If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks. ================================================================================

ned immediately for revision. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation.
