value. The probability refers to the long-run behavior of the procedure, not to this specific interval. This distinction matters when you use confidence intervals to argue for or against agreement between a measurement and a model prediction, which is exactly what you will do in the Week 3 comparative analysis memo. If you have encountered the Bayesian alternative — where a credible interval really does express a probability that the true value lies within a range — that is a different framework; both are valid, but this course uses the frequentist confidence interval because it is the standard in engineering reporting under ASME and ISO measurement standards. ================================================================================ slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility

============================================================================= slide 8: Repeatability vs. Reproducibility (Image: A two-column comparison table with four rows. Column headers: "Repeatability" (blue) and "Reproducibility" (gold). Row 1 "Conditions": "Same operator, same instrument, same lab, short time interval" vs. "Different operators, different instruments, different labs, longer intervals." Row 2 "What it tests": "Instrument precision and procedural consistency" vs. "Whether the result generalizes beyond this specific setup." Row 3 "Typical metric": "Standard deviation of repeated runs within one session" vs. "Standard deviation across operators, labs, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory

, or sessions." Row 4 "Professional standard": "ASME — within-laboratory repeatability" vs. "ISO/ASME — inter-laboratory reproducibility." A note at the bottom: "Always report which you computed. They are not the same claim.") Talking Point: Repeatability and reproducibility are both consistency measures, but they answer different questions. Repeatability is about whether you — same person, same instrument, same session — get the same answer when you repeat a measurement. It tests your instrument's precision and your own procedural discipline. Reproducibility asks whether different people, with different instruments, in different facilities, get the same answer. Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement

Reproducibility is a claim about generalizability. In professional laboratory practice — and in ASME and ISO measurement standards — both are formally defined and both matter for establishing the credibility of an experimental result. In this course, most of your work will assess repeatability, because we are working in a single lab environment. But I want you to know that reproducibility is the higher standard — it is the one that matters when someone who was not in this room needs to trust your result. ================================================================================ slide 9: The Three-Way Distinction in Practice (Image: A three-row decision flowchart. Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale

Row 1: "I observe scatter in my repeated measurements." Three labeled branches emerge. Branch A: "Does the scatter scale with N (reduce when averaging)?" → "Random error dominates — take more measurements." Branch B: "Is the scatter consistent with my instrument spec?" → "Within spec — characterize and report it as uncertainty." Branch C: "Is there a consistent offset from the expected value regardless of N?" → "Systematic error — find the source, correct or bound it." All three branches converge on a final node: "State your result as: Estimate ± Uncertainty (confidence level, method)." Caption: "The distinction is diagnostic — it tells you what action to take.") Talking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you

alking Point: Let me pull this all together with a decision framework you can use when examining your own data. When you observe scatter in repeated measurements, the first question is what kind of variation it is. If the scatter reduces as you take more measurements, you have a random-error-dominated system — averaging helps. If the scatter is consistent with your instrument's datasheet specification, it is within expectation — characterize it and propagate it as uncertainty. If there is a consistent offset from the expected value that does not go away with more measurements, you have a systematic error — stop taking data and find the source. All three scenarios end in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that

d in the same place: you quantify your uncertainty and express your result as an engineering claim. But the path to that destination depends on correctly diagnosing what you are seeing. This is the skill we build in the lab work over the next several weeks. ================================================================================ slide 10: Why This Week's Distinction Drives Everything That Follows (Image: A course arc diagram showing five week nodes on a horizontal timeline — W1 through W5 — connected by arrows. W1: "Design" — AMVF icon. W2: "Uncertainty Tools" — sigma and distribution icon (current week, highlighted in gold). W3: "Quantitative Comparison" — two overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging

wo overlapping error bars icon. W4: "Full Pipeline" — integration icon. W5: "Cross-Prediction" — two students exchanging data icon. Below each arrow, a label shows how the current week's content feeds forward: W2→W3: "Without uncertainty, comparison is meaningless." W3→W4: "Without comparison framework, integration is undisciplined." W4→W5: "Without full pipeline, cross-prediction cannot be executed." The gold highlight on W2 with forward arrows emphasizes that this is the foundational week.) Talking Point: I want to be direct about why this week's content matters beyond Week 2. The three-way distinction you learned today — variability, measurement error, and uncertainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to

rtainty — is the foundation on which every subsequent analysis in this course rests. Next lecture, you will learn how to propagate uncertainty through calculations — how to turn the uncertainty on your raw measurements into the uncertainty on a derived quantity. Week 3 will use that propagated uncertainty to argue whether a measurement and a model prediction actually agree. Week 4 will require you to execute the full pipeline and document your uncertainty at every step. And Week 5 will ask you to estimate the uncertainty in an experiment you have never run, using only a sensor spec sheet. All of that rests on what you are learning today. If the distinction between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be.

between variability, error, and uncertainty is fuzzy for you, the rest of the course will be harder than it needs to be. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — What Is Missing from This Conclusion? (Image: A bar graph showing three side-by-side bars on a white background. Blue bars labeled "Measured Power" at three voltage levels (3V, 6V, 9V) with values approximately 1.8 W, 7.1 W, 16.4 W. Orange bars labeled "Model Prediction (P = V²/R)" at the same voltage levels. The bars appear close in height. No error bars appear on any bar. Below the figure: a caption reading "Measured power agrees well with the model prediction across all three voltage conditions." A bold prompt below: "Is this conclusion defensible? What is

the model prediction across all three voltage conditions." A bold prompt below: "Is this conclusion defensible? What is missing?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this figure and caption. [Pause — allow student responses.] The conclusion is not defensible. Without uncertainty bounds on the bars, you cannot determine whether the visual agreement is meaningful or coincidental. The gap between each blue and orange bar might exceed the measurement uncertainty — in which case you have a discrepancy requiring explanation. Or the gap might be negligible — in which case agreement is supported. But you cannot tell which from this figure. The word "agrees" requires a numerical comparison within stated uncertainty bounds, applied using a pre-committed metric. Without uncertainty

uires a numerical comparison within stated uncertainty bounds, applied using a pre-committed metric. Without uncertainty intervals on every bar, the conclusion has no engineering basis. A professional report that made this claim would be returned immediately for revision. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything. Carl Richards meant that as a caution about overconfidence — about the gap between what you planned for and what actually happened. In experimental engineering, that gap has a name: uncertainty. And the discipline of this course is learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible

learning how to quantify that gap honestly, propagate it through your analysis rigorously, and use it to make defensible engineering claims. Today you have the vocabulary — variability, random error, systematic error, uncertainty, confidence interval, repeatability, reproducibility. Next lecture, you will use it to build a computational skill: error propagation. Come prepared to do math, because that is how the vocabulary becomes a tool. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - Opening quote "Error is what's left over after you think you've thought of everything" — Carl Richards — incorporated in the title slide talking point per the W2 L1 blueprint. - Three-way distinction

ything" — Carl Richards — incorporated in the title slide talking point per the W2 L1 blueprint. - Three-way distinction (variability / measurement error / uncertainty) is the organizing spine of the lecture, as specified in the blueprint Purpose statement. - Confidence interval interpretation (slide 7) uses the correct frequentist framing — students need this for the W3 comparison work. - Repeatability vs. reproducibility (slide 8) references ASME and ISO standards, consistent with the professional standards thread from Week 1. - Slide 10 explicitly connects this lecture forward to W3, W4, and W5 — AMVF continuity maintained without forcing it. - Monte Carlo content deferred to W2 L2 per blueprint structure. - Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor

erred to W2 L2 per blueprint structure. - Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — missing uncertainty in bar graph claim). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 9): Slide 5 talking point — added sentence attributing experimental uncertainty requirements to ASME PTC 19.1, distinguishing from ASME V&V 10-2006 which is the computational modeling standard. - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 6 talking point — added t-distribution note for N < 30 with MATLAB reference. Slide 7 talking point — added one sentence on Bayesian credible interval as an alternative framework, noting course uses frequentist CI per ASME/ISO standards.

pages.title: L02
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Lecture L02 E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ======================================... E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities (Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.") Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively. ================================================================================ slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method (Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.) Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point. ================================================================================ slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms (Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.") Talking Point: I want to use electrical power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental engineering: the same physical quantity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your measurement errors. Power equals IV. Power equals V-squared over R. Power equals I-squared times R. All three are valid equations for electrical power. But if I propagate uncertainty through each one, I get different total output uncertainty — because the partial derivatives are different. In Form 2, voltage appears squared, so errors in voltage are amplified. In Form 3, current appears squared. If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one. ================================================================================ slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest (Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.") Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly. ================================================================================ slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break (Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.") Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding. ================================================================================ slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions (Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian Input Distribution" — bell curve centered on the mean, labeled "Use when: errors are random, symmetric, and instrument precision is characterized as a standard deviation." Right panel: "Uniform (Flat) Input Distribution" — rectangular distribution between lower and upper bounds, labeled "Use when: only tolerance limits are known (e.g., ±X% from datasheet) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P distributions — one from Gaussian inputs, one from flat inputs — with the Gaussian-input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be honest about what you actually know.") Talking Point: When you run a Monte Carlo simulation, you choose input distributions, and that choice matters. Gaussian is appropriate when your errors are random and symmetric and when the instrument precision is characterized well enough to specify a standard deviation. Uniform is the honest choice when all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no information about how errors are distributed within that range. In that case, a flat distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you invented. The output distributions in both cases will look roughly Gaussian by the central limit theorem, but their widths will differ. Choosing the right input distribution is not a technical detail — it is a statement about how much you actually know about your instrument, and intellectual honesty about that is part of sound uncertainty analysis. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where Multiple Models Converge (Image: A single probability density plot showing three overlapping Gaussian curves on the same horizontal axis. The three curves are labeled "P from IV," "P from V²/R," and "P from I²R." The curves have different widths and are slightly offset. The region where all three curves substantially overlap is shaded green and labeled "Region of maximum overlap — best estimate of true P." A vertical dashed line marks the "True Value" falling within the overlap region. An annotation: "Overlap is evidence of consistency. Non-overlap is a diagnostic signal.") Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get three output distributions. Because all three equations are valid models for the same physical quantity, the true value of power must lie in the region where all three distributions overlap. The overlap region is where multiple independent lines of evidence converge, and that convergence is more convincing than any single model alone. If the three distributions substantially overlap, you have internal consistency — your measurements are coherent. If they do not overlap — if two equation forms give incompatible estimates — something is wrong: your measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the first thing to look for when you generate these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward. ================================================================================ slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework (Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.") Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward. ================================================================================ slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example (Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose? (Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.") Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). Kathryn's graph presentation framework added as dedicated slides 9 and 10 — a major new element in Iteration 5 not present in the prior version. Blueprint specifies this framework should be in W2 L2 and provided as a reading in W3. V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Slides 9-10 retained per instructor APPROVE WITH MODIFICATIONS; motivating transition sentence added to slide 9 talking point: "Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive." P=IV, P=V²/R, P=I²R used as working examples throughout, per blueprint explicit instruction. Gaussian vs. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint. Monte Carlo as complement to partial derivative method, overlap region as key output — per blueprint. "Where would you spend your money/budget" framing embedded in slide 5 sensitivity matrix. Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — formula choice based on sensor uncertainties). V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 3 talking point — added worked partial derivative calculation step: ∂P/∂V = 2V/R for P = V²/R, evaluated at V = 12 V, R = 4.8 Ω, showing sensitivity coefficient of 5 W/V. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively. ================================================================================ slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method (Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.) Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point. ================================================================================ slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms (Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.") Talking Point: I want to use electrical power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental engineering: the same physical quantity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your measurement errors. Power equals IV. Power equals V-squared over R. Power equals I-squared times R. All three are valid equations for electrical power. But if I propagate uncertainty through each one, I get different total output uncertainty — because the partial derivatives are different. In Form 2, voltage appears squared, so errors in voltage are amplified. In Form 3, current appears squared. If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one. ================================================================================ slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest (Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.") Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly. ================================================================================ slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break (Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.") Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding. ================================================================================ slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions (Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian Input Distribution" — bell curve centered on the mean, labeled "Use when: errors are random, symmetric, and instrument precision is characterized as a standard deviation." Right panel: "Uniform (Flat) Input Distribution" — rectangular distribution between lower and upper bounds, labeled "Use when: only tolerance limits are known (e.g., ±X% from datasheet) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P distributions — one from Gaussian inputs, one from flat inputs — with the Gaussian-input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be honest about what you actually know.") Talking Point: When you run a Monte Carlo simulation, you choose input distributions, and that choice matters. Gaussian is appropriate when your errors are random and symmetric and when the instrument precision is characterized well enough to specify a standard deviation. Uniform is the honest choice when all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no information about how errors are distributed within that range. In that case, a flat distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you invented. The output distributions in both cases will look roughly Gaussian by the central limit theorem, but their widths will differ. Choosing the right input distribution is not a technical detail — it is a statement about how much you actually know about your instrument, and intellectual honesty about that is part of sound uncertainty analysis. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where Multiple Models Converge (Image: A single probability density plot showing three overlapping Gaussian curves on the same horizontal axis. The three curves are labeled "P from IV," "P from V²/R," and "P from I²R." The curves have different widths and are slightly offset. The region where all three curves substantially overlap is shaded green and labeled "Region of maximum overlap — best estimate of true P." A vertical dashed line marks the "True Value" falling within the overlap region. An annotation: "Overlap is evidence of consistency. Non-overlap is a diagnostic signal.") Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get three output distributions. Because all three equations are valid models for the same physical quantity, the true value of power must lie in the region where all three distributions overlap. The overlap region is where multiple independent lines of evidence converge, and that convergence is more convincing than any single model alone. If the three distributions substantially overlap, you have internal consistency — your measurements are coherent. If they do not overlap — if two equation forms give incompatible estimates — something is wrong: your measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the first thing to look for when you generate these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward. ================================================================================ slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework (Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.") Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward. ================================================================================ slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example (Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose? (Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.") Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - Kathryn's graph presentation framework added as dedicated slides 9 and 10 — a major new element in Iteration 5 not present in the prior version. Blueprint specifies this framework should be in W2 L2 and provided as a reading in W3. V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Slides 9-10 retained per instructor APPROVE WITH MODIFICATIONS; motivating transition sentence added to slide 9 talking point: "Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive." - P=IV, P=V²/R, P=I²R used as working examples throughout, per blueprint explicit instruction. - Gaussian vs. flat variance distinction covered in slide 7 per blueprint. - Monte Carlo as complement to partial derivative method, overlap region as key output — per blueprint. - "Where would you spend your money/budget" framing embedded in slide 5 sensitivity matrix. - Slide count: 12 (title + 9 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — formula choice based on sensor uncertainties). - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 3 talking point — added worked partial derivative calculation step: ∂P/∂V = 2V/R for P = V²/R, evaluated at V = 12 V, R = 4.8 Ω, showing sensitivity coefficient of 5 W/V.

certainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.") Talking Point: Last, we're going to look at how to present a graph so your uncertainty argument is visible — see the Week 3 reading for a deeper dive. I want to teach you how to present a graph. Every MATLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step

TLAB figure you produce in this course needs to do more than display data — it needs to make an argument. The eight-step framework on this slide is the structure I will hold you to on every figure caption and every verbal presentation of data. Step one: why does this graph exist? What question is it answering? Every figure should have a reason to exist. Step two: axes — what is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale? Step three: data source — where did the data come from? Step four: uncertainties — are error bars or uncertainty bounds visible and labeled? Step five: what trend does the data show? Step six: how does the trend compare to the predictive model? Step seven: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you

: what are the possible causes for any discrepancy between data and model? And step eight: what does this graph tell you about the overall system — not just the immediate measurement? This framework will be reinforced as a required reading in Week 3, and it applies to every figure from this point forward. ================================================================================ slide 10: Applying the Framework — A Worked Example (Image: A sample MATLAB-style figure showing electrical power (y-axis, in Watts) vs. input voltage (x-axis, in Volts) for the P = V²/R model. Blue dots represent measured data with vertical error bars (showing propagated uncertainty). A solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars,

solid red line shows the model prediction P = V²/R. The figure has a title, labeled axes with units, visible error bars, and a legend distinguishing data from model. Below the figure, the eight-step checklist is applied in annotation form: "(1) This graph tests whether P scales as V² for fixed R. (2) Axes: Power [W] vs. Voltage [V]. (3) Data: multimeter measurements, Lab 1. (4) Uncertainties: propagated via partial derivative method. (5) Trend: power increases with voltage squared. (6) Model: P = V²/R shown as solid line. (7) Discrepancy: data at high voltage slightly above model — possible heating effect on resistance. (8) System: resistance increases with temperature, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like

re, invalidating fixed-R assumption at high currents.") Talking Point: Let me walk through what the framework looks like applied to a real graph from Lab 1. The figure shows power versus voltage for the P equals V-squared over R model. I start with motivation: this graph tests whether power scales as the square of voltage for a fixed resistance — a core prediction of the model. Axes are labeled with units. Data source is the multimeter measurements you'll collect today. Uncertainties are shown as error bars, computed by partial derivative propagation. The trend is upward and curved, consistent with a squared relationship. The model line fits well at low voltage. At high voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature

voltage, data trends slightly above the model — step seven: possible cause is that resistance increases with temperature at high current, which means the fixed-R assumption is no longer valid. Step eight — system implication: if resistance is temperature-dependent, the P equals I-squared-R form with measured R may not be the best model at elevated current. That is an engineering insight. That is what the graph is for. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluator Moment — Which Formula Would You Choose? (Image: A clean prompt box on a white background. Text: "Your instruments: voltmeter uncertainty = ±5% of reading. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the

ing. Ammeter uncertainty = ±1% of reading. Resistance from datasheet: ±2%. You need to compute electrical power with the smallest possible total uncertainty. Which formula — P = IV, P = V²/R, or P = I²R — should you use, and why? Do not compute — just judge." A small table shows the three formulas and space for the student's reasoning. A prompt: "You have 90 seconds.") Talking Point: Take 90 seconds and make a judgment — not a calculation. [Pause.] The answer is P = I²R. Here is the reasoning. In P = V²/R, voltage appears squared — so the squared sensitivity coefficient amplifies the largest uncertainty (5% on voltage). That is the worst choice given these instruments. In P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current

n P = IV, voltage and current contributions add in quadrature — the 5% voltage term still dominates. In P = I²R, current is squared but current has the smallest uncertainty (1%), so the amplification applies to the smallest input. The 2% resistance term also enters, but that is still smaller than the 5% voltage contribution in the other forms. The key point: sensitivity analysis is a design tool. You can choose the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your worst sensor — but only if you understand what the partial derivatives are doing. That reasoning is what the oral interview will ask for. ================================================================================ slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than

============= slide 12: Closure Error propagation is the skill that makes uncertainty analysis computational rather than conceptual. By computing partial derivatives, you trace exactly how errors in your raw measurements amplify as they flow through your equations — and that tells you where the dominant uncertainty comes from and where effort is best spent. Monte Carlo gives you a computational check that does not require linearity assumptions. Both methods end in the same place: a result expressed as an estimate plus its uncertainty interval, at a stated confidence level, with the dominant source identified. And when you present that result in a figure, the eight-step framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured,

framework is how you make the argument visible — so that someone reading your report can see not just what you measured, but what it means. What you build today — the habit of asking where uncertainty comes from, quantifying it, and presenting it in a way that makes an argument — is something you will carry through every experiment for the rest of your engineering career. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). E1_W2_L2 — Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 2, Lecture 2 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Talking

============================== slide 1: Title Slide Title: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo Talking Point: Last lecture gave you the conceptual vocabulary — variability, error, uncertainty, and why the three-way distinction matters. Now I want to make uncertainty analysis computational. The core challenge is this: in most experiments, the quantity you care about is not what you measure directly — it is something you calculate from multiple raw measurements, each carrying its own uncertainty. Force from voltage. Power from current and voltage. Velocity from position and time. When your inputs are uncertain, how does that uncertainty flow through the calculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you

alculation into the output? That question is what error propagation answers. And once you can propagate uncertainty, you need to communicate it — which is why this lecture also covers how to present a graph that makes your uncertainty visible and your argument legible. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem — Uncertainty in Derived Quantities (Image: A schematic flow diagram on a white background. On the left, three labeled input boxes: "V = 12.0 ± 0.3 V," "I = 2.5 ± 0.1 A," "R = 4.8 ± 0.2 Ω." Arrows from each input converge on a central gray function box labeled "P = f(V, I, R) — model determines form." From the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty

rom the function box, a right-pointing arrow leads to an output box: "P = ? ± ?" with a question mark on the uncertainty interval. Below the diagram: "Each input has uncertainty. The output inherits combined uncertainty — but how much, and from which inputs?" A small annotation on the arrows: "The sensitivity of the output to each input depends on the function form.") Talking Point: Here is the situation you will face in almost every experiment in this course. You measure several quantities — voltage, current, resistance, angle, period — and you compute a derived quantity from them. Each measured input has uncertainty. Your derived output inherits uncertainty from all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input

rom all of your inputs, combined through the functional form of your equation. The question is how much, and which input contributes the most. This matters practically: if you know which input is the dominant source of uncertainty in your output, you know where to invest effort — better instrumentation, more measurements, tighter control — to reduce the total output uncertainty most efficiently. Error propagation is the tool that answers this question quantitatively. ================================================================================ slide 3: First-Order Error Propagation — The Partial Derivative Method (Image: A derivation box on a clean white background. Header: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ =

eader: "Derived quantity Q = f(x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ)." Below: the first-order propagation formula in large, clean type: "δQ = √[(∂f/∂x₁ · δx₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂ · δx₂)² + ...]." A labeled decomposition shows each term "(∂f/∂xᵢ · δxᵢ)" with two annotations: the ∂f/∂xᵢ term labeled "Sensitivity coefficient — how much Q changes per unit of xᵢ" and δxᵢ labeled "Input uncertainty." A callout box: "Assumptions: (1) Input errors are independent. (2) Errors are small relative to input values — first-order approximation is valid." The formula is readable from the back of a classroom.) Talking Point: The partial derivative method is the formal tool for propagating uncertainty through a function. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the

nction. The logic is: if Q depends on multiple uncertain inputs, then the uncertainty in Q is approximately equal to the square root of the sum of squares of each input's contribution — where each contribution is the sensitivity coefficient for that input multiplied by the uncertainty in that input. The sensitivity coefficient is the partial derivative of Q with respect to that input — it tells you how much the output changes per unit change in the input. A large sensitivity coefficient on a noisy input is the signature of a dominant error source. The method assumes independence between inputs and that errors are small enough for the first-order linear approximation to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in

to hold. Both assumptions are reasonable for most engineering scenarios, and we will see what happens when they break in more complex problems later in the course. To make this concrete: for P = V²/R, the partial derivative with respect to V is ∂P/∂V = 2V/R — which means a 1 V error in a 12 V reading with R = 4.8 Ω contributes (2×12/4.8)×δV = 5 × δV to the uncertainty in P. That factor of 5 is the sensitivity coefficient, and it depends entirely on the operating point. ================================================================================ slide 4: The Power Trio — Same Quantity, Three Equation Forms (Image: Three separate boxed equations arranged horizontally on a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" —

n a clean white background. Box 1: "P = IV" — labeled "Form 1." Box 2: "P = V²/R" — labeled "Form 2." Box 3: "P = I²R" — labeled "Form 3." Below each box, a stacked bar chart shows the fractional uncertainty contribution from each input for representative values (V = 12 V ± 2%, I = 2.5 A ± 4%, R = 4.8 Ω ± 3%). The bars are color-coded by input: blue for V, orange for I, green for R. The relative heights of the colored segments differ dramatically across the three forms — Form 2 has V dominating, Form 3 has I dominating. Bold caption: "Same physical quantity. Different equation form. Different sensitivity to input errors.") Talking Point: I want to use electrical power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental

l power as the working example because it makes something visible that is true of every derived quantity in experimental engineering: the same physical quantity can be expressed by multiple different equation forms, and the different forms have different sensitivities to your measurement errors. Power equals IV. Power equals V-squared over R. Power equals I-squared times R. All three are valid equations for electrical power. But if I propagate uncertainty through each one, I get different total output uncertainty — because the partial derivatives are different. In Form 2, voltage appears squared, so errors in voltage are amplified. In Form 3, current appears squared. If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the

If your voltmeter is less precise than your ammeter, these forms give you different total uncertainty in P. Choosing the equation form that minimizes sensitivity to your most uncertain input is a real engineering decision — not a cosmetic one. ================================================================================ slide 5: Sensitivity Analysis — A Map for Where to Invest (Image: A decision matrix styled as a 2x2 quadrant diagram. X-axis labeled "Sensitivity Coefficient (partial derivative magnitude)" running left (low) to right (high). Y-axis labeled "Input Uncertainty δxᵢ" running bottom (low) to top (high). Four quadrant labels: upper-right "HIGH PRIORITY — big sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control

ig sensitivity, big uncertainty. Fix this first." Upper-left "Monitor — big sensitivity, small uncertainty. Good control — maintain it." Lower-right "Investigate — small sensitivity despite large uncertainty. May not matter." Lower-left "Don't worry — small sensitivity, small uncertainty." Three labeled dots show where V, I, and R fall for a specific P = IV example, with V in the upper-left and I in the upper-right. Caption: "This matrix is a design tool — use it before you run the experiment, not only after.") Talking Point: The sensitivity breakdown from error propagation is not just a reporting exercise — it is a design tool. The most dangerous inputs to your derived quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those

quantity are the ones that combine high sensitivity — large partial derivative — with high uncertainty — large δx. Those are the inputs in the upper right of this matrix, and those are where you should focus your instrumentation budget, your calibration effort, and your additional measurements. High sensitivity with low uncertainty means you have done a good job controlling something important — do not let that slip. High uncertainty with low sensitivity means the input is imprecise but it barely moves your output — you have flexibility there. This framing applies to every experiment you will run in this course: before you set up hardware, you should be asking which input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain

h input variables contribute most to the uncertainty in the quantity you care about, and designing the measurement chain accordingly. ================================================================================ slide 6: Monte Carlo Simulation — When the Assumptions Break (Image: A four-panel flow diagram showing Monte Carlo steps. Panel 1: "Define Input Distributions" — three Gaussian curves for V, I, R with labeled means and standard deviations. Panel 2: "Draw N = 10,000 Random Samples" — a scatter cloud of sample triplets (V, I, R). Panel 3: "Compute P for Each Sample" — shown as a MATLAB-style code snippet: "P_samples = V_samples .* I_samples;" and the resulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve

ulting output array. Panel 4: "Plot Output Distribution" — a histogram of computed P values with a fitted Gaussian curve overlaid. A caption below: "Monte Carlo does not require small-error or linearity assumptions — it directly samples the full nonlinear function.") Talking Point: The partial derivative method is powerful and computationally efficient, but it has two assumptions: errors must be small enough for the first-order approximation to hold, and inputs must be independent. When those assumptions fail — when your function is highly nonlinear, or when uncertainty is large relative to the mean — Monte Carlo simulation gives you a more reliable answer. The concept is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples

pt is simple: you define probability distributions for each of your input measurements, draw thousands of random samples from those distributions, compute the derived quantity for every sample, and look at the distribution of outputs. The spread of that output distribution is your uncertainty estimate, without any linearity assumption. MATLAB makes this straightforward — a loop over ten thousand samples runs in milliseconds — and this week's lab is built around implementing it. The important result to look for is whether the Monte Carlo output and the partial derivative output agree. When they do, the partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding.

e partial derivative method is validated. When they diverge, you have identified a nonlinear region worth understanding. ================================================================================ slide 7: Gaussian vs. Flat Input Distributions (Image: A two-panel side-by-side comparison. Left panel: "Gaussian Input Distribution" — bell curve centered on the mean, labeled "Use when: errors are random, symmetric, and instrument precision is characterized as a standard deviation." Right panel: "Uniform (Flat) Input Distribution" — rectangular distribution between lower and upper bounds, labeled "Use when: only tolerance limits are known (e.g., ±X% from datasheet) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P

et) and you do not know the distribution shape within that range." Below both panels, a third panel showing two output P distributions — one from Gaussian inputs, one from flat inputs — with the Gaussian-input result slightly narrower. Caption: "Your choice of input distribution changes your output uncertainty estimate. Be honest about what you actually know.") Talking Point: When you run a Monte Carlo simulation, you choose input distributions, and that choice matters. Gaussian is appropriate when your errors are random and symmetric and when the instrument precision is characterized well enough to specify a standard deviation. Uniform is the honest choice when all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no

all you know is the datasheet tolerance — the instrument is rated to within ±2% of reading, for example, but you have no information about how errors are distributed within that range. In that case, a flat distribution from minus-2% to plus-2% is more defensible than assuming a Gaussian with a specific standard deviation you invented. The output distributions in both cases will look roughly Gaussian by the central limit theorem, but their widths will differ. Choosing the right input distribution is not a technical detail — it is a statement about how much you actually know about your instrument, and intellectual honesty about that is part of sound uncertainty analysis. ================================================================================ slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where

================================================================================ slide 8: The Overlapping Region — Where Multiple Models Converge (Image: A single probability density plot showing three overlapping Gaussian curves on the same horizontal axis. The three curves are labeled "P from IV," "P from V²/R," and "P from I²R." The curves have different widths and are slightly offset. The region where all three curves substantially overlap is shaded green and labeled "Region of maximum overlap — best estimate of true P." A vertical dashed line marks the "True Value" falling within the overlap region. An annotation: "Overlap is evidence of consistency. Non-overlap is a diagnostic signal.") Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get

s a diagnostic signal.") Talking Point: When you run Monte Carlo simulations for the three power equation forms, you get three output distributions. Because all three equations are valid models for the same physical quantity, the true value of power must lie in the region where all three distributions overlap. The overlap region is where multiple independent lines of evidence converge, and that convergence is more convincing than any single model alone. If the three distributions substantially overlap, you have internal consistency — your measurements are coherent. If they do not overlap — if two equation forms give incompatible estimates — something is wrong: your measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the

measurements are inconsistent, or one of your models is invalid for these conditions. The overlap-or-not question is the first thing to look for when you generate these plots in the lab today, and it is the visual analogue of the validation question the whole course builds toward. ================================================================================ slide 9: How to Present a Graph — Kathryn's Framework (Image: A numbered eight-step checklist formatted as a clean vertical list with consistent iconography. Each step has a number, a short label, and a one-line description. (1) Motivation — "Why does this graph exist? What question does it answer?" (2) Axes — "What is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?"

t is plotted, with units, at appropriate scale?" (3) Data Source — "Where did the data come from? How was it collected?" (4) Uncertainties — "Error bars or uncertainty bounds shown and labeled?" (5) Trend — "What pattern does the data show?" (6) Model Comparison — "How does the trend compare to the predictive model?" (7) Discrepancy — "What are possible causes for any gap between data and model?" (8) System-Level Implication — "What does this graph tell you about the overall system?" Each step has a small icon to the left. A caption at the bottom: "Apply this checklist to every MATLAB figure you produce in this course.")
