pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Reading R01 # Uncertainty Analysis Reference — Coleman & Steele, 4th Edition ## A Field Guide for Engineers Who Take Their Results Seriously The moment you accept that every measurement is a distribution — not a number — your ent... Uncertainty Analysis Reference — Coleman & Steele, 4th Edition A Field Guide for Engineers Who Take Their Results Seriously The moment you accept that every measurement is a distribution — not a number — your entire relationship with experimental data changes. This reading introduces a reference text you will use repeatedly throughout this course: Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers by Hugh W. Coleman and W. Glenn Steele, Fourth Edition. It is not a cover-to-cover assignment. It is a field guide — different chapters address different problems you will encounter, and this reading orients you to the book's structure so you can find what you need when you need it. Coleman & Steele belongs to a tradition of engineering texts that treat measurement uncertainty not as an afterthought — the error bar you add before submitting a figure — but as the primary product of an experiment. The core thesis of the book matches the core thesis of this course: the goal of an experiment is not to produce a number, it is to produce a defensible claim. A defensible claim requires not just a result but a quantified bound on how uncertain you are about that result, derived by methods that a critical reader can evaluate and reproduce. <image description: A two-panel illustration. Left panel shows a single point on a number line labeled "reported value: 9.81 m/s²" — clean, falsely precise, with no additional information. Right panel shows a bell curve centered on 9.81 m/s² with shaded tails labeled "±0.04 m/s² (95% CI, partial derivative propagation)" — the same measurement expressed as a distribution with explicit confidence bounds and method noted. A caption beneath both panels reads: "One of these is an observation. The other is an engineering claim." Black-on-white illustration, textbook diagram style with clean typography.> Part I of Coleman & Steele (Chapters 1–4) is the section most relevant to E1. Chapter 1 establishes the conceptual framework: the three-way distinction between elemental error sources (individual contributors to uncertainty), random errors (scatter that reduces with repeated measurements), and systematic errors (bias that does not reduce with repetition). This structure and its vocabulary are exactly what Week 2's lectures develop. If a concept from lecture feels imprecise, Chapter 1 is the first place to check — it provides the careful definitions and worked examples that lecture time does not always allow. Chapter 3 is where the partial derivative propagation method is fully derived. If you have seen the formula for propagating uncertainty through a derived quantity — summing the squared products of partial derivatives and input uncertainties in a root-sum-of-squares combination — that formula is the central result of this chapter. Coleman & Steele derive it carefully, identify the assumptions it requires (first-order Taylor series expansion, uncorrelated error sources), and work through engineering examples of the same form you will encounter in Lab 1. The three power equations — P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R — are precisely the kind of derived-quantity example this chapter addresses. Different equation forms for the same physical quantity yield different partial derivatives and therefore different sensitivities to the same input errors. Chapter 3 is where that result is grounded in theory. Chapter 4 introduces Monte Carlo simulation as a complement to the partial derivative method. Where partial derivatives give you a closed-form estimate of output uncertainty given input distributions, Monte Carlo simulation draws many samples from those input distributions and propagates them through the full equation, revealing the output distribution directly. The two methods agree when the assumptions of partial derivative propagation hold well; they diverge when the output function is highly nonlinear or when input distributions are non-Gaussian. Week 2 develops both methods in the context of Lab 1 — Coleman & Steele Chapter 4 is the theoretical grounding. If the lecture derivation moved quickly, Chapter 4 is where to find the slower version: the assumptions are spelled out, the edge cases are discussed, and worked examples show exactly where the two methods converge and where they diverge. <image description: A side-by-side diagram comparing two uncertainty propagation methods. Left side, labeled "Partial Derivative Method": a mathematical expression showing the RSS combination of (∂f/∂x₁·u₁)² + (∂f/∂x₂·u₂)² with an arrow to a single bell curve labeled "Predicted output distribution (analytical)." Right side, labeled "Monte Carlo Simulation": an arrow from "Input distributions (sampled N=10,000 times)" flowing into a function block labeled "f(x₁, x₂, ...)" and out to a histogram of simulated outputs labeled "Empirical output distribution." A bracket below both sides reads "Both methods estimate the same quantity — check them against each other." Clean technical diagram, MATLAB-inspired plot style, CU Boulder gold accent on axis labels.> Part II of Coleman & Steele addresses validation — comparing experimental outcomes to model predictions within uncertainty bounds. This section becomes more relevant in E2 and E3, but it is worth noting now: the same reference you use for uncertainty propagation in Week 2 also develops the conceptual framework for the quantitative comparison methods you will use in Week 3 and beyond. The book carries you through the full measurement-to-validation pipeline. The relationship between Coleman & Steele and the ASME standards from Week 1 is deliberate: the book's methodology is consistent with ASME PTC 19.1 (the measurement uncertainty standard for performance testing) and with the uncertainty quantification framework assumed by ASME V&V 10-2006. The international reference for measurement uncertainty is the ISO GUM ( Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement ); Coleman & Steele's methods are consistent with the GUM framework, making the book an accessible entry point into what is otherwise a dense international standard. Applying Coleman & Steele's methods in your lab reports means applying an approach that satisfies recognized professional standards — which matters when engineering results are submitted for design decisions, regulatory review, or publication. The Takeaway: Coleman & Steele is the methodological backbone behind every uncertainty propagation exercise in this course — keep it as a reference, not a reading, and use it to ground your understanding in the professional standards that this course's framework is built on. Chapters 1 and 3 are your Week 2 anchors; Chapter 4 supports Monte Carlo; Part II becomes relevant in Week 3 and beyond.

pages.title: R02
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Reading R02 # Technical Writing in Engineering ## Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping — not hedging, not over-ex... Technical Writing in Engineering Say What You Mean, Prove What You Say The hardest thing about technical writing is not grammar. It is the discipline of making a claim and then stopping — not hedging, not over-explaining, not padding — and letting the evidence speak for itself. You have been writing for years. You know how to tell a story, argue a point, describe something you observed. Technical writing uses none of those skills in quite the same way. It is a different genre with different rules, and the rules exist for a specific reason: engineering writing is not read for enjoyment. It is read by someone who needs to make a decision, verify a result, reproduce a measurement, or check your work. Every sentence exists to support that purpose, or it should not exist at all. This reading gives you the practical framework for engineering writing that you will apply throughout this course — starting with the Lab 1 brief report this week. The Reader You Are Writing For Before writing a single sentence, ask yourself: who reads this, and what do they need from it? In professional engineering, your reader is a technical person who has never seen your specific work before. They are competent — they understand the physics, the methods, the vocabulary. They are not, however, clairvoyant. They cannot infer what you measured from how you describe your setup. They cannot reconstruct your uncertainty calculation from a result with no error bars. They cannot evaluate your conclusion without knowing how you got there. Write for the skeptical expert, not the sympathetic friend. The sympathetic friend fills in gaps; the skeptical expert points at them. The goal is a document that survives the skeptical expert: one where every claim is supported, every result is reproducible, and no reader has to guess at anything essential. A second audience worth naming explicitly: the non-engineer. Several assignments in this course ask you to explain a technical result to someone without an engineering background. This is not dumbing down — it is a different technical skill. Plain-language communication requires you to identify the essential idea underneath the technical apparatus and express it without jargon. If you cannot do that, it is a signal that your own understanding is not yet complete. <image description: A two-panel illustration. Left panel, labeled "The Sympathetic Friend," shows a reader leaning forward, nodding, filling in a thought bubble with a complete version of an incomplete sentence: the written text trails off mid-sentence but the reader's thought bubble finishes it generously. Right panel, labeled "The Skeptical Expert," shows a reader with a red pen, question marks in their thought bubble, circling the same incomplete sentence and writing "Insufficient detail — how was this measured?" Clean editorial illustration style, black and white with one accent color.> Worked Example: From Engineering Form to Plain Language Here is the same result written two ways. Engineering form (for the skeptical expert): > "The measured electrical power dissipated in a 5 Ω resistor with a 12 V input is 25.7 ± 1.3 W at 95% confidence, derived using partial derivative uncertainty propagation from voltage and current measurements with a ±0.2 V voltmeter and ±0.05 A ammeter." This version is correct and complete. Every number is supported. Every bound is traceable to a method. A colleague can reproduce the result. Plain-language version (for the non-engineer): > "We measured how much electrical power a small resistor was consuming when connected to a 12-volt source — similar to a standard lantern battery. The result was about 26 watts, roughly the energy a dim incandescent bulb uses. Our measurement has an uncertainty of about 5%, which means the true value is almost certainly somewhere between 24 and 27 watts." What changed and why: Uncertainty expressed as consequence, not as a statistical bound. "±1.3 W at 95% confidence" is meaningless to a non-engineer; "about 5%" gives a sense of scale, and "almost certainly between 24 and 27 watts" states the consequence in plain language. Physical result grounded in an everyday reference. "25.7 W" is a number; "roughly the energy a dim incandescent bulb uses" is a physical anchor that makes the magnitude interpretable. Technical method removed. The non-engineer reader does not need to know about partial derivative propagation — they need to trust that the measurement was done carefully. That trust is built through the framing ("our measurement has an uncertainty of about 5%"), not through the method details. The skeptical expert version and the plain-language version contain the same core result. Writing both is not redundant — it is evidence that you understand the result well enough to translate it. Several deliverables in this course require both: the Lab 1 brief report, the Phase 1 report (Section 9), and the Phase 3 presentation. The Architecture of a Technical Document Every technical document — lab report, memo, technical note — is a narrative. Not a story with plot and character, but a logical argument that has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Readers orient by structure. A document without clear structure is a document that wastes the reader's time. The standard structure for an engineering report maps directly onto the logical arc of a technical argument: Introduction — Three things, in this order: (1) what this document is about and why it was written; (2) what new information it contains that the reader did not have before; (3) why the results matter. The introduction is not background for its own sake — it is a contract with the reader about what they are about to receive. Body — The evidence: your method, your measurements, your analysis. Sufficient detail that someone else could reproduce your results. This is where your figures, tables, and equations live — each one integrated into the narrative, not appended to it. Conclusion — Not a summary of the introduction. A conclusion states: (1) what was found; (2) what that finding implies; (3) what remains uncertain or unresolved; (4) what comes next. Conclusions that merely restate the introduction have done nothing. If a section of your report does not serve one of these three purposes, it probably should not be there. Figures: The Core Unit of Technical Communication Most technical writing errors are not in the prose — they are in the figures and in the prose that surrounds them. A figure in an engineering report is not a visual aid. It is an argument. It exists to support a specific claim, and every element of the figure must serve that claim. When you produce any figure in this course, the following elements are required — not optional: Labeled axes with units. "Force (N)" not "Force." "Time (s)" not "seconds." No axis is unlabeled, no unit is omitted. A caption that identifies and explains. The caption must name every plotted element and explain what the figure shows. "Figure 2. Measured power vs. voltage for all three configurations" is a label. "Figure 2. Measured power as a function of applied voltage for the P = IV, P = V²/R, and P = I²R configurations. Error bars represent ±1σ from partial-derivative uncertainty propagation. The three configurations converge at low voltages but diverge above 5V, consistent with resistance variation as a function of temperature." — that is a caption. Reference in the text. Every figure is referenced before it appears. "Figure 2 shows..." or "as shown in Figure 2..." — never a figure floating in the document without a textual anchor that explains why it is there. Conclusions drawn explicitly. Do not leave the reader to extract the point. After referencing the figure, state what it shows, what it implies, and whether any anomalies require explanation. <image description: Two versions of the same MATLAB-style scatter plot with a best-fit line. Top version, labeled "Insufficient," has unlabeled axes ("x" and "y"), no units, a title that says "Data," and no caption below. Bottom version, labeled "Sufficient," has the same data but with fully labeled axes ("Applied Voltage (V)" and "Measured Power (W)"), a legend, error bars on data points, a figure number, and a two-sentence caption that names the trend and identifies what the error bars represent. Same data, entirely different level of communication. Clean technical diagram style.> The Language of Technical Writing Engineering writing has a distinctive voice — and it is not stuffy or passive by accident. The conventions exist because precision matters more than personality. No contractions. "Do not" not "don't." "Cannot" not "can't." This is not pedantry — contractions are a signal of informality that undermines technical credibility in a professional document. Define before you use. Every acronym is spelled out on first use: "National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)." Every non-obvious symbol is defined. Assume the reader can look up a word; do not assume they will know what your variable names mean. Be specific. Vague claims invite skepticism. "The measurement was close to the predicted value" tells the reader nothing useful. "The measured power (4.32 W) fell within the 95% confidence interval of the propagated uncertainty estimate (4.18 to 4.51 W)" makes a falsifiable claim. Every quantitative result should be stated with its uncertainty in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level). Active voice, lean sentences. "The apparatus was connected, and three sets of data were collected" is cleaner than "I plugged in the apparatus and successfully collected three sets of data." The passive voice is appropriate when the actor is irrelevant or unknown — not as a default. The goal is directness, not impersonality. Remove what does not earn its place. Technical writing is edited ruthlessly. Words like "very," "basically," "actually," "in order to," "it should be noted that," and "as previously mentioned" add length without adding meaning. The revision process is not about polishing prose — it is about cutting everything that does not serve the reader's need to understand your result. Numbers, Equations, and Units Numbers and equations follow conventions that are enforced uniformly in professional engineering writing — and will be enforced in this course. Space between number and unit. Write 9.81 m/s² not 9.81m/s² . Write 2.4 mm not 2.4mm . No exceptions. Consistent units. Do not switch between metric and imperial mid-document. If your data is in N, your results are in N. Variables are italicized; numbers and units are not. The variable P represents power; the value 4.32 W is a measurement. Only variables are italicized. Equations are part of the text. Introduce every equation in a sentence: "Power dissipated in the resistor is given by..." Equations are not standalone objects — they are statements that the surrounding prose must set up and interpret. Every result carries uncertainty. A number without uncertainty bounds is not an engineering result — it is a reading. State method, confidence level, and bounds every time. The Revision Mindset Good technical writing is not written — it is revised. The document you produce in the first pass is a draft that organizes your thinking, not a product that communicates it. Every professional communicator — engineers included — treats revision as the actual work. A practical three-pass approach: First pass , check structure — does the document have a clear introduction, body, and conclusion that form a complete logical argument? Second pass , check claims — is every quantitative statement supported? Is every figure correctly referenced and captioned? Is every conclusion drawn explicitly from evidence? Third pass , check language — are there contractions? Vague terms? Redundant words? Sentences that could be cut in half without losing meaning? This course's use of AI for drafting does not change this framework — it intensifies it. An AI-generated draft may have perfect grammar and confident tone while being subtly wrong about the physics, imprecise about the uncertainty, or logically disconnected in its conclusions. Your job is not to produce the draft — it is to know what right looks like so you can recognize when the draft is wrong. The revision mindset is the professional skill this course is building. <image description: A horizontal flow diagram with three labeled stages. Stage 1, "Draft," shows a rough document icon with a thought bubble that says "organized my ideas." Stage 2, "Revision," shows a document with red ink marks, strikethroughs, and margin notes — labels point to specific types of edits: "structural," "claims," "language." Stage 3, "Final," shows a clean document icon with a checkmark. An arrow beneath all three stages reads: "The revision IS the work." Flat illustration style, clean typography, CU Boulder gold accent color on the arrow.> A Checklist for Every Deliverable Before submitting any written work in this course, confirm: [ ] Every figure has labeled axes with units, a complete caption, and an explicit reference in the text [ ] Every quantitative result is stated in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level) [ ] The introduction states purpose, new information, and significance [ ] The conclusion states findings, implications, uncertainties, and next steps — not a restatement of the introduction [ ] No contractions appear anywhere in the document [ ] Every acronym is defined on first use; every symbol is defined before use [ ] There is a space between every number and its unit [ ] Units are consistent throughout [ ] Every claim that needs evidence has it [ ] The document has been read aloud at least once — sentences that are hard to say aloud are hard to read The Takeaway: Technical writing is not a soft skill bolted onto engineering — it is the mechanism by which your results become claims that others can evaluate, reproduce, and build on. A result that cannot be communicated clearly is a result that does not yet exist as engineering knowledge. CLO Alignment: CLO 5 — Communicate experimental results effectively using standard technical writing conventions Assigned: E1, Week 2 | Companion deliverable: Lab 1 Brief Report Sources: Write More Good Club Technical Writing Checklist (UTEP/Rumpf Lab); Technical Writer's Checklist, TSC 2007; ASEN 2003 Lab Report Guide (Scheeres/Park, CU Boulder)

The three configurations converge at low voltages but diverge above 5V, consistent with resistance variation as a function of temperature." — that is a caption. Reference in the text. Every figure is referenced before it appears. "Figure 2 shows..." or "as shown in Figure 2..." — never a figure floating in the document without a textual anchor that explains why it is there. Conclusions drawn explicitly. Do not leave the reader to extract the point. After referencing the figure, state what it shows, what it implies, and whether any anomalies require explanation. <image description: Two versions of the same MATLAB-style scatter plot with a best-fit line. Top version, labeled "Insufficient," has unlabeled axes ("x" and "y"),

e MATLAB-style scatter plot with a best-fit line. Top version, labeled "Insufficient," has unlabeled axes ("x" and "y"), no units, a title that says "Data," and no caption below. Bottom version, labeled "Sufficient," has the same data but with fully labeled axes ("Applied Voltage (V)" and "Measured Power (W)"), a legend, error bars on data points, a figure number, and a two-sentence caption that names the trend and identifies what the error bars represent. Same data, entirely different level of communication. Clean technical diagram style.> The Language of Technical Writing Engineering writing has a distinctive voice — and it is not stuffy or passive by accident. The conventions exist because precision matters more than personality. No contractions. "Do not" not "don't." "Cannot" not

e conventions exist because precision matters more than personality. No contractions. "Do not" not "don't." "Cannot" not "can't." This is not pedantry — contractions are a signal of informality that undermines technical credibility in a professional document. Define before you use. Every acronym is spelled out on first use: "National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)." Every non-obvious symbol is defined. Assume the reader can look up a word; do not assume they will know what your variable names mean. Be specific. Vague claims invite skepticism. "The measurement was close to the predicted value" tells the reader nothing useful. "The measured power (4.32 W) fell within the 95% confidence interval of the propagated uncertainty estimate (4.18 to 4.51 W)" makes a falsifiable claim.

l within the 95% confidence interval of the propagated uncertainty estimate (4.18 to 4.51 W)" makes a falsifiable claim. Every quantitative result should be stated with its uncertainty in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level). Active voice, lean sentences. "The apparatus was connected, and three sets of data were collected" is cleaner than "I plugged in the apparatus and successfully collected three sets of data." The passive voice is appropriate when the actor is irrelevant or unknown — not as a default. The goal is directness, not impersonality. Remove what does not earn its place. Technical writing is edited ruthlessly. Words like "very," "basically," "actually," "in order to," "it should be noted that," and "as previously mentioned" add length

like "very," "basically," "actually," "in order to," "it should be noted that," and "as previously mentioned" add length without adding meaning. The revision process is not about polishing prose — it is about cutting everything that does not serve the reader's need to understand your result. Numbers, Equations, and Units Numbers and equations follow conventions that are enforced uniformly in professional engineering writing — and will be enforced in this course. Space between number and unit. Write 9.81 m/s² not 9.81m/s² . Write 2.4 mm not 2.4mm . No exceptions. Consistent units. Do not switch between metric and imperial mid-document. If your data is in N, your results are in N. Variables are italicized; numbers and units are not. The variable P represents power; the value 4.32 W is a

ts are in N. Variables are italicized; numbers and units are not. The variable P represents power; the value 4.32 W is a measurement. Only variables are italicized. Equations are part of the text. Introduce every equation in a sentence: "Power dissipated in the resistor is given by..." Equations are not standalone objects — they are statements that the surrounding prose must set up and interpret. Every result carries uncertainty. A number without uncertainty bounds is not an engineering result — it is a reading. State method, confidence level, and bounds every time. The Revision Mindset Good technical writing is not written — it is revised. The document you produce in the first pass is a draft that organizes your thinking, not a product that communicates it. Every professional

roduce in the first pass is a draft that organizes your thinking, not a product that communicates it. Every professional communicator — engineers included — treats revision as the actual work. A practical three-pass approach: First pass , check structure — does the document have a clear introduction, body, and conclusion that form a complete logical argument? Second pass , check claims — is every quantitative statement supported? Is every figure correctly referenced and captioned? Is every conclusion drawn explicitly from evidence? Third pass , check language — are there contractions? Vague terms? Redundant words? Sentences that could be cut in half without losing meaning? This course's use of AI for drafting does not change this framework — it intensifies it. An AI-generated draft may

ning? This course's use of AI for drafting does not change this framework — it intensifies it. An AI-generated draft may have perfect grammar and confident tone while being subtly wrong about the physics, imprecise about the uncertainty, or logically disconnected in its conclusions. Your job is not to produce the draft — it is to know what right looks like so you can recognize when the draft is wrong. The revision mindset is the professional skill this course is building. <image description: A horizontal flow diagram with three labeled stages. Stage 1, "Draft," shows a rough document icon with a thought bubble that says "organized my ideas." Stage 2, "Revision," shows a document with red ink marks, strikethroughs, and margin notes — labels point to specific types of edits: "structural,"

a document with red ink marks, strikethroughs, and margin notes — labels point to specific types of edits: "structural," "claims," "language." Stage 3, "Final," shows a clean document icon with a checkmark. An arrow beneath all three stages reads: "The revision IS the work." Flat illustration style, clean typography, CU Boulder gold accent color on the arrow.> A Checklist for Every Deliverable Before submitting any written work in this course, confirm: [ ] Every figure has labeled axes with units, a complete caption, and an explicit reference in the text [ ] Every quantitative result is stated in engineering form: estimate ± uncertainty (units, method, confidence level) [ ] The introduction states purpose, new information, and significance [ ] The conclusion states findings, implications,

[ ] The introduction states purpose, new information, and significance [ ] The conclusion states findings, implications, uncertainties, and next steps — not a restatement of the introduction [ ] No contractions appear anywhere in the document [ ] Every acronym is defined on first use; every symbol is defined before use [ ] There is a space between every number and its unit [ ] Units are consistent throughout [ ] Every claim that needs evidence has it [ ] The document has been read aloud at least once — sentences that are hard to say aloud are hard to read The Takeaway: Technical writing is not a soft skill bolted onto engineering — it is the mechanism by which your results become claims that others can evaluate, reproduce, and build on. A result that cannot be communicated clearly is a

sults become claims that others can evaluate, reproduce, and build on. A result that cannot be communicated clearly is a result that does not yet exist as engineering knowledge. CLO Alignment: CLO 5 — Communicate experimental results effectively using standard technical writing conventions Assigned: E1, Week 2 | Companion deliverable: Lab 1 Brief Report Sources: Write More Good Club Technical Writing Checklist (UTEP/Rumpf Lab); Technical Writer's Checklist, TSC 2007; ASEN 2003 Lab Report Guide (Scheeres/Park, CU Boulder)

pages.title: lab1 PIV
pages.text: E1 Week 2 (Course Week 2) Assessment lab1 PIV # ASEN 3501 — Lab 1: Power, Voltage, and the Language of Uncertainty ## Student-Facing Assignment **Segment:** E1 — Execution & Quantification **Week:** 2 **CLO Alignment:** CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 5 **Assessment:** Brief l... ASEN 3501 — Lab 1: Power, Voltage, and the Language of Uncertainty Student-Facing Assignment Segment: E1 — Execution & Quantification Week: 2 CLO Alignment: CLO 1, CLO 2, CLO 5 Assessment: Brief lab report (support document) + 3-minute oral interview Estimated time: 3–5 hours total (in-lab activity: ~1 hour; report preparation: 2–4 hours) Why This Lab Exists Every engineering measurement you will ever take comes with a number and an uncertainty. The number without the uncertainty is incomplete — it tells you what the instrument displayed, not what the physical quantity actually is or how confident you should be in that display. This lab is your first chance to practice treating uncertainty as a required output, not an afterthought. The physical setup is deliberately simple: a resistor, a power supply, and a multimeter. The simplicity is intentional. There is nothing here to distract you from the core question: given that your instruments have limits, what can you actually claim about the power dissipated in this circuit? That question — and the rigorous way to answer it — is the foundation on which everything else in this course is built. Learning Goals By the end of this lab and its associated activities, you should be able to: Measure voltage and current using a digital multimeter and correctly read and record the result — including an appropriate uncertainty assigned from the instrument datasheet. Apply first-order uncertainty propagation (the general method using partial derivatives) to compute the uncertainty in a derived quantity — electrical power — from measurements of voltage and current. Explain why different algebraic forms of the same physical law (P = IV, P = V²/R, P = I²R) produce different uncertainty estimates for the same quantity, and articulate which form is most sensitive to which measured input. Run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB to estimate uncertainty using the same manufacturer specifications, and compare the Monte Carlo result to the partial-derivative result. State a result in the correct engineering form: *estimate ± uncertainty (units, confidence level or method)*. Explain the core concepts of this lab clearly and concisely to someone who has not taken this course. Background: The Circuit You will build a simple series circuit: a DC power supply, a known resistor, and a multimeter. You will measure: V — voltage across the resistor (voltmeter, in parallel) I — current through the resistor (ammeter, in series) From these measurements you will compute electrical power three ways: Equation Measured inputs What you derive P = IV I and V directly Power from both measurements P = V²/R V and R (nominal) Power using voltage and resistance P = I²R I and R (nominal) Power using current and resistance Each form uses different measured inputs. Different inputs have different uncertainties. Different forms therefore produce different uncertainty estimates for the same quantity P. This is not an error — it is the physics of error propagation. The Instrument — Reading Your Multimeter What the display is telling you A digital multimeter display shows a numerical reading that is updated at a fixed rate. On most benchtop multimeters, the least significant digit — the rightmost digit — will fluctuate during a measurement. This is not noise in the measurement. It is the instrument's resolution limit combined with real variation in the signal. The rule for recording measurements from a fluctuating display: > Cover the fluctuating digit with your finger. Read and record the stable digits. Assign > an uncertainty of ±½ of the last stable digit's place value. Example: Your voltmeter reads 4.9_3_, 4.9_2_, 4.9_4_ — the units digit in the tenths place is stable (4.9) but the hundredths digit fluctuates (2, 3, 4). You record 4.9 V and assign an uncertainty of ±0.05 V (half of one unit in the tenths place). This is a conservative but not overcautious recording strategy. You are not ignoring variation — you are bounding it at the resolution of the instrument. The tradeoff matters: if you assign unnecessarily large uncertainties, the systems designed around your measurements will be over-engineered and expensive. If you assign uncertainties that are too small, those systems will fail. The correct answer is the smallest defensible bound. Using the datasheet The instrument datasheet specifies accuracy in the form: ± (% of reading + number of counts) For example: ± (0.5% of reading + 2 counts). This is the manufacturer specification for the maximum systematic error. You will use this specification — not the fluctuating digit alone — as your formal uncertainty estimate for the partial-derivative and Monte Carlo analyses. You will receive the multimeter datasheet at the start of the lab session. Before you begin any measurements, locate the specification for: DC voltage accuracy in the range you are using DC current accuracy in the range you are using Write these down. They are your primary uncertainty inputs. In-Lab Procedure Part 1 — Build and measure Build the series circuit using the provided resistor, power supply, and multimeter. Record the nominal resistor value and tolerance from its label. Set the power supply to a target voltage (specified by your lab instructor). Read and record V and I from the multimeter displays using the fluctuating-digit procedure above. Record each reading three times to check for repeatability. Compute P three ways from your recorded values. Note the differences. Part 2 — Uncertainty from the datasheet Using the manufacturer specifications from the datasheet, compute the uncertainty in V and the uncertainty in I. Apply first-order error propagation (partial derivatives) to compute the uncertainty in P for each of the three forms. Show your work: write out the partial derivatives, substitute values, and state the result as P ± u_P. Answer: which form of the power equation is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Where would you spend money on a better instrument if minimizing uncertainty in P was the goal? Part 3 — Monte Carlo in MATLAB Using the same manufacturer specifications as probability distribution parameters, run a Monte Carlo simulation in MATLAB for each of the three power equation forms. Model V and I as independent random variables drawn from distributions consistent with the datasheet (Gaussian or uniform — justify your choice). Use at least 10,000 samples. Plot the resulting distribution of P for each form on the same figure with appropriate labels and captions. Compare the Monte Carlo result to your partial-derivative result. Are they consistent? If not, explain why they might differ. Deliverable: Brief Lab Report You will prepare a brief lab report to bring to your oral interview. This document is your support material for the interview — you may refer to it during the conversation, but the interview questions will probe your understanding, not your ability to read your own document. The report is not graded independently. It is evidence that you have prepared. Required sections 1. Circuit description and measurement record One paragraph and a simple schematic or sketch. What did you build? What did you measure? Include your recorded V and I values with units. Include the datasheet specification you used. 2. Fluctuating digit — what you did and why Describe the fluctuating digit procedure in your own words. What did the display show? What did you record? What uncertainty did you assign from the display, and why? 3. Uncertainty propagation — partial derivatives Show the three partial derivative derivations for u_P under each power equation form. Present the final result for each as P ± u_P with units. One table or three clearly labeled calculations is acceptable. 4. Sensitivity analysis Which form is most sensitive to uncertainty in V? In I? Answer with numbers, not just words. Where would you invest in a better instrument, and why? 5. Monte Carlo simulation Include your MATLAB figure(s) showing the distributions of P for each equation form. Each figure must have a title, labeled axes with units, a legend, and a caption that states the key takeaway in one sentence. Include the key MATLAB code (the simulation loop and distribution plot — not every setup line). 6. Comparison: partial derivatives vs. Monte Carlo Are the results consistent? State the comparison numerically — do not just say "yes they agree." What does the comparison tell you about when you might prefer one method over the other? 7. Result statement State your best estimate of P in the correct engineering form: P = [value] ± [uncertainty] [units] ([confidence level or method, e.g., 95% CI or manufacturer spec bound]) 8. Plain-language explanation In 3–5 sentences, explain what you did in this lab and what your result means to someone who has never taken an engineering course. Assume your audience is a curious high school student. This section should be in your introduction or conclusion — not appended as an afterthought. Oral Interview The oral interview is a 3-minute, one-on-one conversation with a member of the teaching team. You may bring your lab report. No other materials are permitted. The interview is a pass/fail assessment. You must pass this interview to satisfy the Lab 1 requirement. See the separate file LAB1_PIV_rubric_student.md for the question categories and what a passing response looks like. Prepare by reviewing that document before your interview. Preparing with the course AI tutor The course AI tutor persona is described in the E0 supplemental materials. You can upload this assignment document and the student rubric to a Claude or ChatGPT session configured with the tutor persona, and practice your oral responses before the interview. Example prompts: *"Ask me an oral interview question about why P = IV and P = V²/R give different uncertainty estimates."* "Pretend you are the instructor and ask me to explain the fluctuating digit procedure." *"Give me a harder version of a question about when Monte Carlo is preferable to the partial derivative method."* The AI tutor cannot predict the exact questions you will be asked, but it can give you targeted practice on the core concepts. Use it. Resources E1 Week 2 Lecture: Error Propagation, Sensitivity Analysis & Monte Carlo E1 Week 2 Lecture: Variability, Error, and Uncertainty Coleman & Steele, Experimentation, Validation, and Uncertainty Analysis for Engineers , 4th ed. — Chapter on uncertainty propagation (reference copy on course page) Multimeter datasheet — provided at lab session MATLAB starter script — available on Canvas (sets up the Monte Carlo loop structure; you complete the equation forms and plotting) E0 Supplemental: Virtual Multimeter Simulator (optional bonus activity) E0 Supplemental: AI Tutor Setup and Practice For multimeter to measure current and voltage simultaneous: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y6xnLkiUMn8 ASEN 3501 — Lab 1 Assignment | E1 Week 2 Evergreen — physics content; Update-friendly — multimeter datasheet specifications, MATLAB starter script, power supply target voltage

E1_W3_L1 — Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics ASEN 3501: Aerospace Experimental Methods | Week 3, Lecture 1 Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint — 2026-05-14 ================================================================================ slide 1: Title Slide Title: Structured Comparison & Validation Metrics Talking Point: Weeks 1 and 2 gave you the tools to measure a quantity and express your confidence in it as an uncertainty interval. Week 3 asks the next question: now that you have a measurement with quantified uncertainty and a model prediction, how do you decide whether they agree? Not visually — not by eyeballing a plot and saying "they look close" — but with a structured, defensible, numerical argument. That shift, from visual judgment to structured comparison, is one of the most important cognitive moves in this course. This lecture gives you the tools to make it, including how to put your comparison criteria directly on the figure where anyone reading the report can see them. ================================================================================ slide 2: The Problem with Visual Inspection (Image: Three side-by-side plots showing identical data and model, differing only in axis scale. Plot 1: axes zoomed far out — data points and model line appear to coincide perfectly. Plot 2: axes zoomed tightly — data and model appear to diverge dramatically. Plot 3: intermediate scale — judgment is genuinely ambiguous. All three plots have the same axis labels and the same data values; only the scale differs. A caption below: "Same data. Same model. Three different visual impressions. Which one represents agreement?" A red annotation: "Agreement cannot depend on how you made the figure.") Talking Point: I want you to feel this problem before we solve it. I have shown you the same dataset and the same model prediction three times, with three different axis scales. In the first plot, they look identical. In the second, they look like significant disagreement. In the third, you are genuinely unsure. But the data and model have not changed — only the axis scale has. This is the fundamental problem with visual inspection as the basis for an engineering conclusion. A plot is a presentation choice, and presentation choices are not physics. If your conclusion about agreement changes depending on how you drew the figure, then your conclusion is not grounded in the data — it is grounded in aesthetics. The rest of this lecture is about replacing aesthetic judgment with a defined numerical protocol. ================================================================================ slide 3: What Does "Agreement" Mean Technically? (Image: A horizontal number line diagram. A vertical dashed line labeled "Model Prediction μ_model" is shown at a fixed position, with a light shaded band around it labeled "Model Uncertainty ±U_model." A measurement point labeled "Experimental Outcome μ_exp" is shown with a horizontal two-headed arrow labeled "±U_exp." Three cases are stacked vertically below the number line. Case A: the two uncertainty bands overlap broadly — green label "Overlap — agreement plausible." Case B: the bands just barely touch — yellow label "Marginal — quantify carefully." Case C: the bands are fully separated — red label "No overlap — genuine discrepancy requires explanation." A note: "Agreement depends on both uncertainties, not just one.") Talking Point: Agreement in the engineering sense means that the difference between your measurement and your model prediction is not larger than what can be explained by the combined uncertainty of both quantities. Not just the measurement uncertainty — both. A model prediction carries uncertainty too, from approximations, parameter assumptions, and model-form limitations. The question is not "is the measurement equal to the prediction?" but "is the difference between measurement and prediction consistent with zero, given the combined uncertainty of both?" When uncertainty intervals overlap, agreement is plausible. When they do not overlap at all, you have a genuine discrepancy that requires explanation — either the model is wrong, the measurement is wrong, or your uncertainty estimates are wrong. And "plausible" is not enough — you still need to quantify it with a specific metric. ================================================================================ slide 4: Validation Metrics — Name Your Criterion Before You Look (Image: Three labeled formula boxes arranged vertically on a clean white background. Box 1: "Relative Difference: d_rel = |Q_exp − Q_model| / Q_model × 100%. Interprets as: how far apart are the values as a percentage?" Box 2: "Within Combined Uncertainty: |Q_exp − Q_model| < √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: is the difference smaller than the combined uncertainty at the stated confidence level?" Box 3: "Normalized Agreement Index: NAI = |Q_exp − Q_model| / √(U_exp² + U_model²). Interprets as: how many combined standard deviations apart are the two values? NAI < 1 suggests agreement." A red text box at the bottom: "REQUIRED: state your metric and acceptance threshold BEFORE computing the comparison. Post-hoc metric selection is not defensible.") Talking Point: A validation metric is a defined, reproducible procedure for quantifying the distance between a measurement and a model prediction in units that account for the relevant uncertainties. Three representative forms are shown here. Relative difference is the simplest — it gives you the percentage gap — but it does not account for uncertainty. The combined-uncertainty check adds the critical test: is the gap smaller than what your uncertainties can explain? The normalized agreement index generalizes this by expressing disagreement in units of combined standard uncertainty — values below one suggest agreement. The critical rule is at the bottom: you must state your metric and acceptance threshold before you look at the result. If you choose your metric after seeing the data — post-hoc, meaning after the fact — you can always find one that confirms what you hoped to find. That is a well-documented bias in experimental reporting, and it is why pre-commitment is required in every deliverable in this course. The term "post-hoc" is worth knowing: it appears in academic literature, review comments, and professional engineering standards, and recognizing it will help you identify this specific failure mode when you see it in your own work or someone else's. ================================================================================ slide 5: A Worked Comparison — Full Numerical Example (Image: A structured step-by-step worked example box. Measurement: "Q_exp = 14.7 N, U_exp = ±0.4 N (95% CI, partial derivative method)." Model prediction: "Q_model = 15.3 N, U_model = ±0.6 N (from model parameter uncertainty)." Step 1: "Relative difference: |14.7 − 15.3| / 15.3 × 100% = 3.9%." Step 2: "Combined uncertainty: U_comb = √(0.4² + 0.6²) = 0.72 N = 4.7% of model prediction." Step 3: "Comparison: 3.9% < 4.7% — the difference is within combined uncertainty." Step 4 (highlighted in gold): "Conclusion: Agreement is supported at 95% confidence using the relative-difference-within-combined-uncertainty metric, pre-committed before this calculation." A note at the bottom: "All steps must appear in your report in this order.") Talking Point: Let me walk through a complete worked example so you see exactly what the report section looks like. I have a force measurement of 14.7 N with a 95% uncertainty interval of plus or minus 0.4 N, and a model prediction of 15.3 N with an estimated model uncertainty of plus or minus 0.6 N. Step one: compute the relative difference — 3.9%. Step two: compute the combined uncertainty as the quadrature sum of the two individual uncertainties — 0.72 N, or 4.7% of the model value. Step three: compare — 3.9% is less than 4.7%, so the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. Step four: state the conclusion, tied to the metric and the confidence level. Every step must appear in your report, in this order. Skipping steps, or stating the conclusion without showing the calculation, is insufficient documentation for an engineering comparison. ================================================================================ slide 6: When Agreement Fails — Disagreement Is Diagnostic (Image: A two-branch flowchart starting from "Comparison Metric Exceeds Threshold." Branch 1: "Measurement problem?" — sub-nodes: "Calibration error? Installation effect? Uncontrolled confounder?" Branch 2: "Model problem?" — sub-nodes: "Wrong equation form? Invalid assumption? Missing physical effect?" Both branches converge at "Investigate root cause — then classify the discrepancy type." Below: two residual plots. Left: residuals randomly scattered around zero — labeled "Random residuals: consistent with measurement noise." Right: residuals trending upward with the independent variable — labeled "Structured residuals: evidence of missing physics, not noise.") Talking Point: When your validation metric tells you that measurement and model do not agree within your defined criterion, that is not a failed experiment — it is a finding. The disagreement is diagnostic: it tells you that either your measurement has an unaccounted-for problem or your model is missing something. Your job is to diagnose which. The residuals — defined as: residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point — carry that diagnostic information. Random residuals that scatter around zero with no pattern suggest measurement noise. Residuals that show a systematic trend — always positive, or increasing with the independent variable — suggest a model-form limitation: the model is missing a physical effect that grows with that variable. Structured disagreement is more useful than agreement, because it tells you something specific about what the model is missing. ================================================================================ slide 7: Comparing Two Measurements — The Multi-Sensor Case (Image: Two side-by-side numerical scenarios. Scenario A: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 14.2 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 14.2| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 0.5 / 0.64 = 0.78 — within 1 combined σ. Agreement supported." Below: green checkmark. Scenario B: "Sensor 1: 14.7 ± 0.4 N. Sensor 2: 16.1 ± 0.5 N. |14.7 − 16.1| / √(0.4² + 0.5²) = 1.4 / 0.64 = 2.19 — exceeds 2 combined σ. Disagreement: investigate sensor calibration or installation." Below: red annotation. A caption: "The same metric applies whether you are comparing measurement to model or measurement to measurement.") Talking Point: The same validation metric framework applies when you are comparing two measurements of the same quantity using different sensors — which is exactly what the lab activity this week requires. The calculation is identical: compute the difference between the two sensor readings, compute the combined uncertainty, ask whether the difference falls within the combined uncertainty. If yes, the two sensors are internally consistent — you have cross-validated one against the other. If no, at least one sensor has a problem: calibration error, installation effect, or a systematic difference in what the two sensors are physically responding to. Using two independent sensors to measure the same quantity is one of the most powerful tools in experimental practice, because independent evidence is more convincing than a single instrument, and this lab is designed specifically around that. ================================================================================ slide 8: Showing Margins and Requirements on Plots (Image: A clean figure showing experimental data (blue dots with error bars) and a model prediction (solid red curve) on a plot with labeled axes. In addition to the data and model, three horizontal reference lines are added: a solid gold line labeled "Requirement: P ≤ 35 W," a dashed green line labeled "Design Margin: P ≤ 30 W," and a light gray shaded band between 30 and 35 W labeled "Margin Zone." Several data points fall below 30 W (in the "pass" region), one falls in the margin zone, and none exceed 35 W. A caption: "The plot itself communicates whether the result passes or fails — no text narrative required.") Talking Point: A brief but important practical skill: when your experiment is connected to a design requirement — a load limit, a temperature bound, an efficiency threshold — you can and should annotate your figure with that requirement directly. Put a horizontal line on the plot at the requirement value. Add a second line at the design margin if one exists. Now the figure communicates the conclusion without requiring the reader to cross-reference a separate document: every data point is visually classified as passing or failing. This technique connects your experimental result to the engineering decision the experiment was designed to inform. It is not a deep dive into systems design — that is for other courses — but it is a practical communication skill that will appear in every technical report you produce in industry. In this course, when your experiment has a defined acceptance criterion, that criterion should appear on the figure. ================================================================================ slide 9: Building a Defensible Comparison Argument — The Required Structure (Image: A structured six-step outline formatted as a numbered list, styled like a report section template. "(1) State validation metric — name it, explain why it is appropriate for this data type." "(2) State acceptance threshold — defined before the comparison." "(3) Apply metric numerically — show all intermediate calculations." "(4) State conclusion — agreement or disagreement — tied to the confidence level and the stated metric." "(5) If disagreement: describe residual structure — random or systematic?" "(6) If agreement: note the conditions under which agreement holds and any caveats about range or extrapolation." A red annotation at steps 1 and 2: "Pre-commitment required — these two steps must precede step 3.") Talking Point: I want to give you the required structure for a defensible comparison argument, because this structure will appear in every deliverable from this point forward. First: name your validation metric and justify why it is appropriate. Different data types and different modeling claims call for different metrics. Second: state your acceptance threshold before computing. Third: show the full numerical calculation with all intermediate steps. Fourth: state the conclusion explicitly — agreement or disagreement — tied to the metric and the confidence level. Fifth: if the comparison reveals disagreement, classify the residual structure. And sixth: if the comparison supports agreement, note the conditions and limits of that agreement. This is the argument structure for the Comparative Analysis Memo due this week, and it is the same structure I will look for in every subsequent lab report. ================================================================================ slide 10: The AMVF Comparison Node — What You Are Doing (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the "Quantitative Comparison" node highlighted prominently in gold. Arrows entering the node from the left are labeled "Simulation Outcomes (with model uncertainty)" and from the right "Experimental Outcomes (with measurement uncertainty)." Arrows leaving the node: one labeled "Agreement within criterion → Validation supported" pointing upward-left toward the Mathematical Model node, and one labeled "Disagreement → Investigate model-form or measurement problem" pointing downward. A bracket below the node reads "This is the activity you are executing in the Week 3 lab and memo. This is where the two branches of the AMVF meet.") Talking Point: I want to locate everything in this lecture within the AMVF, because that connection is important. The quantitative comparison node is where the two branches of the AMVF meet — where the simulation output from the mathematical branch and the experimental outcome from the physical branch are evaluated against each other. Everything you have learned in the last two weeks — measurement, uncertainty propagation, confidence intervals — has been preparation for this node. The structured comparison you are now able to execute is not a report-writing exercise. It is the central activity of the validation branch of the AMVF. When you apply your validation metric in the lab this week, you are executing a step that professional engineers perform every time they need to argue that a model is credible for a specific use case and a specific set of conditions. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluating Another's Work — The Four-Part Critique Structure (Image: A clean four-box layout on a white background, each box color-coded. Box 1 (green): "(1) What is correct and well-supported? — Name specific evidence. Identify where the report does what it claims." Box 2 (red): "(2) What is incorrect or unsupported? — Identify claims that are wrong, or conclusions that go beyond what the data shows." Box 3 (gold): "(3) What is missing? — Identify required elements that are absent: uncertainty quantification, pre-committed metric, stated confidence level, or required calculation steps." Box 4 (blue): "(4) What specific improvements would most strengthen this report? — Be actionable. Not 'add more analysis' but 'state the validation metric before applying it and show the intermediate calculation.'" A red caption below: "Name the criteria before you read — for the same reason you commit to a metric before you compute." A note: "This structure applies to the in-class comparative analysis exercise and to every peer evaluation in this course.") Talking Point: In a moment, we are going to apply everything from today's lecture to a sample lab report. Before you open it, I want to give you the four questions that organize a defensible critique — the same way I asked you to commit to a validation metric before computing. First: what is correct and well-supported? Identify what the report does right, with specific evidence. This matters because vague praise is useless — if you cannot say why something is correct, you have not evaluated it. Second: what is incorrect or unsupported? Find claims that are wrong, or conclusions that extend beyond what the data actually shows. Third: what is missing? This is the category most students underuse. Missing elements — no uncertainty quantification, no pre-committed metric, no confidence level stated — are not vague weaknesses; they are specific absences that a checklist can identify. Fourth: what specific improvements would most strengthen the report? Actionable, specific, tied to the assignment requirements. "Add more analysis" is not a critique — "state the validation metric before applying it and show the propagation calculation" is. You are committing to these four criteria now, before you read — for the same reason we commit to a metric before we compute. The quality of your critique will be evaluated on how specifically and accurately you apply all four. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Is This Comparison Defensible? (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's lab report excerpt: "After computing the data, I tried both the relative difference metric and the combined-uncertainty metric. The relative difference showed agreement (4.2% < 5.0% threshold). I used that metric for my conclusion." A bold prompt below: "Is this comparison defensible? What, if anything, is wrong with the approach?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause — allow student responses.] The approach is not defensible. The student computed the data first, tried multiple metrics, and then chose the one that gave agreement. This is post-hoc metric selection — a form of motivated reasoning that allows any dataset to be made to "agree" by selecting the metric that best suits the outcome. The rule is explicit: the metric and acceptance threshold must be stated before the comparison is computed. If the student genuinely believes relative difference is the right metric for this data type, they should be able to explain why on first principles, independent of which metric produces the more favorable answer. The fact that they tried both and chose the favorable one is the problem — not the metric itself. Real credibility requires that the comparison could have shown disagreement, and the student was committed to reporting it regardless. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure Structured comparison is not complicated, but it requires discipline at a moment when most people are tempted to eyeball the result and call it good. The discipline is this: define the metric and commit to the threshold, compute it numerically, show your work, and state the conclusion clearly — including the conditions under which it holds. When the comparison reveals disagreement, read the residuals and diagnose before you conclude. When it supports agreement, annotate your figure with the acceptance criterion so the result is visible to anyone who reads the report. This week's Comparative Analysis Memo is your first practice of this full argument structure. What you build this week is the foundational skill for every validation activity in Phase II and Phase III — and for every engineering report you write after this course is over. ================================================================================ Agent Notes Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). Slide 8 "Showing Margins and Requirements on Plots" is new — incorporates the Iteration 5 blueprint addition: "A brief segment should cover how to show design margins and acceptance criteria on data plots." Kept explicitly scoped as a communication skill, not a systems design topic, per the blueprint instruction. Pre-commitment to metric and threshold emphasized in slides 4 and 9 — matches course assessment philosophy from instructor_clarification_notes. Multi-sensor comparison in slide 7 connects to the W3 lab activity. Residual structure diagnostic introduced in slides 6 and 9 — seeds W3 L2 (calibration vs. validation, anomalous data patterns) without front-loading that content. AMVF comparison node made explicit in slide 10. Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). V&V Executor 2026-05-19: Added slide 11 (Evaluator Moment — post-hoc metric selection); renumbered closure to 12. V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (item 8): Added slide 11 (Four-Part Critique Structure — opens the comparative analysis session). Old slides 11→12 (evaluator moment), 12→13 (closure). Critique structure pre-committed before in-class exercise, mirroring the pre-commitment principle already in slide 4 and 9. V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 4 — added inline definition of "post-hoc" (after the fact) per INSTRUCTOR APPROVES BUT KEEP BOTH. Slide 6 — added explicit definition of "residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point" at first use. First: name your validation metric and justify why it is appropriate. Different data types and different modeling claims call for different metrics. Second: state your acceptance threshold before computing. Third: show the full numerical calculation with all intermediate steps. Fourth: state the conclusion explicitly — agreement or disagreement — tied to the metric and the confidence level. Fifth: if the comparison reveals disagreement, classify the residual structure. And sixth: if the comparison supports agreement, note the conditions and limits of that agreement. This is the argument structure for the Comparative Analysis Memo due this week, and it is the same structure I will look for in every subsequent lab report. ================================================================================ slide 10: The AMVF Comparison Node — What You Are Doing (Image: The AMVF flowchart with the "Quantitative Comparison" node highlighted prominently in gold. Arrows entering the node from the left are labeled "Simulation Outcomes (with model uncertainty)" and from the right "Experimental Outcomes (with measurement uncertainty)." Arrows leaving the node: one labeled "Agreement within criterion → Validation supported" pointing upward-left toward the Mathematical Model node, and one labeled "Disagreement → Investigate model-form or measurement problem" pointing downward. A bracket below the node reads "This is the activity you are executing in the Week 3 lab and memo. This is where the two branches of the AMVF meet.") Talking Point: I want to locate everything in this lecture within the AMVF, because that connection is important. The quantitative comparison node is where the two branches of the AMVF meet — where the simulation output from the mathematical branch and the experimental outcome from the physical branch are evaluated against each other. Everything you have learned in the last two weeks — measurement, uncertainty propagation, confidence intervals — has been preparation for this node. The structured comparison you are now able to execute is not a report-writing exercise. It is the central activity of the validation branch of the AMVF. When you apply your validation metric in the lab this week, you are executing a step that professional engineers perform every time they need to argue that a model is credible for a specific use case and a specific set of conditions. ================================================================================ slide 11: Evaluating Another's Work — The Four-Part Critique Structure (Image: A clean four-box layout on a white background, each box color-coded. Box 1 (green): "(1) What is correct and well-supported? — Name specific evidence. Identify where the report does what it claims." Box 2 (red): "(2) What is incorrect or unsupported? — Identify claims that are wrong, or conclusions that go beyond what the data shows." Box 3 (gold): "(3) What is missing? — Identify required elements that are absent: uncertainty quantification, pre-committed metric, stated confidence level, or required calculation steps." Box 4 (blue): "(4) What specific improvements would most strengthen this report? — Be actionable. Not 'add more analysis' but 'state the validation metric before applying it and show the intermediate calculation.'" A red caption below: "Name the criteria before you read — for the same reason you commit to a metric before you compute." A note: "This structure applies to the in-class comparative analysis exercise and to every peer evaluation in this course.") Talking Point: In a moment, we are going to apply everything from today's lecture to a sample lab report. Before you open it, I want to give you the four questions that organize a defensible critique — the same way I asked you to commit to a validation metric before computing. First: what is correct and well-supported? Identify what the report does right, with specific evidence. This matters because vague praise is useless — if you cannot say why something is correct, you have not evaluated it. Second: what is incorrect or unsupported? Find claims that are wrong, or conclusions that extend beyond what the data actually shows. Third: what is missing? This is the category most students underuse. Missing elements — no uncertainty quantification, no pre-committed metric, no confidence level stated — are not vague weaknesses; they are specific absences that a checklist can identify. Fourth: what specific improvements would most strengthen the report? Actionable, specific, tied to the assignment requirements. "Add more analysis" is not a critique — "state the validation metric before applying it and show the propagation calculation" is. You are committing to these four criteria now, before you read — for the same reason we commit to a metric before we compute. The quality of your critique will be evaluated on how specifically and accurately you apply all four. ================================================================================ slide 12: Evaluator Moment — Is This Comparison Defensible? (Image: A quote box on a clean white background showing a student's lab report excerpt: "After computing the data, I tried both the relative difference metric and the combined-uncertainty metric. The relative difference showed agreement (4.2% < 5.0% threshold). I used that metric for my conclusion." A bold prompt below: "Is this comparison defensible? What, if anything, is wrong with the approach?") Talking Point: Take two minutes and evaluate this claim. [Pause — allow student responses.] The approach is not defensible. The student computed the data first, tried multiple metrics, and then chose the one that gave agreement. This is post-hoc metric selection — a form of motivated reasoning that allows any dataset to be made to "agree" by selecting the metric that best suits the outcome. The rule is explicit: the metric and acceptance threshold must be stated before the comparison is computed. If the student genuinely believes relative difference is the right metric for this data type, they should be able to explain why on first principles, independent of which metric produces the more favorable answer. The fact that they tried both and chose the favorable one is the problem — not the metric itself. Real credibility requires that the comparison could have shown disagreement, and the student was committed to reporting it regardless. ================================================================================ slide 13: Closure Structured comparison is not complicated, but it requires discipline at a moment when most people are tempted to eyeball the result and call it good. The discipline is this: define the metric and commit to the threshold, compute it numerically, show your work, and state the conclusion clearly — including the conditions under which it holds. When the comparison reveals disagreement, read the residuals and diagnose before you conclude. When it supports agreement, annotate your figure with the acceptance criterion so the result is visible to anyone who reads the report. This week's Comparative Analysis Memo is your first practice of this full argument structure. What you build this week is the foundational skill for every validation activity in Phase II and Phase III — and for every engineering report you write after this course is over. ================================================================================ ## Agent Notes - Regenerated from Iteration 5 blueprint (2026-05-14). - Slide 8 "Showing Margins and Requirements on Plots" is new — incorporates the Iteration 5 blueprint addition: "A brief segment should cover how to show design margins and acceptance criteria on data plots." Kept explicitly scoped as a communication skill, not a systems design topic, per the blueprint instruction. - Pre-commitment to metric and threshold emphasized in slides 4 and 9 — matches course assessment philosophy from instructor_clarification_notes. - Multi-sensor comparison in slide 7 connects to the W3 lab activity. - Residual structure diagnostic introduced in slides 6 and 9 — seeds W3 L2 (calibration vs. validation, anomalous data patterns) without front-loading that content. - AMVF comparison node made explicit in slide 10. - Slide count: 13 (title + 10 content + evaluator moment + closure). Old slides 11→12 (evaluator moment), 12→13 (closure). Critique structure pre-committed before in-class exercise, mirroring the pre-commitment principle already in slide 4 and 9. - V&V Executor 2026-05-19 (not-consolidated): Slide 4 — added inline definition of "post-hoc" (after the fact) per INSTRUCTOR APPROVES BUT KEEP BOTH. Slide 6 — added explicit definition of "residual = measured value − model prediction at each data point" at first use.
